blocks.html:
EXPOSÉ III
RELATIVE FINITENESS CONDITIONSby L. Illusie
1. Relative pseudo-coherence

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 1.1.

Let \(n\in\mathbb Z\). For each morphism of schemes \(f:X\longrightarrow Y\) which is locally of finite type, there exists one and only one full subcategory \(D(f)_{n\text{-coh}}\) of \(D(X)\)(1) such that the conditions below are satisfied:

(i) If \(E\in\operatorname{ob}D(f)_{n\text{-coh}}\), then \(i^*(E)\in\operatorname{ob}D(fi)_{n\text{-coh}}\) for every open \(i:U\longrightarrow X\).

(ii) If \(E\in\operatorname{ob}D(X)\) and if there exists an open covering \((i_\alpha:U_\alpha\longrightarrow X)_{\alpha\in A}\) such that \(i_\alpha^*(E)\in\operatorname{ob}D(fi_\alpha)_{n\text{-coh}}\) for every \(\alpha\in A\), then \(E\in\operatorname{ob}D(f)_{n\text{-coh}}\).

(iii) If

\[\xymatrix{ X\ar[r]^f\ar[dr]_h&Y\ar[d]^g\\ &Z }\]

is a commutative triangle of morphisms locally of finite type, where \(f\) is a closed immersion and \(g\) a smooth morphism, then we have

\[E\in\operatorname{ob}D(h)_{n\text{-coh}}\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad f_*(E)\in D(Y)_{n\text{-coh}}{}^{(2)}.\]

Proof. Uniqueness is obvious. Indeed, let \(f:X\longrightarrow Y\) be a morphism locally of finite type. Choose an open covering \((i_\alpha:X_\alpha\longrightarrow X)_{\alpha\in A}\) such that, for each \(\alpha\in A\), \(fi_\alpha\) factors as
\[X_\alpha\xrightarrow{i'_\alpha}Y_\alpha\xrightarrow{f'_\alpha}Y,\]

where \(i'_\alpha\) is a closed immersion and \(f'_\alpha\) a smooth morphism. It follows at once from (i), (ii), (iii) that the objects of \(D(f)_{n\text{-coh}}\) are the objects \(E\) of \(D(X)\) such that \(i'_{\alpha*}i_\alpha^*(E)\in\operatorname{ob}D(Y_\alpha)_{n\text{-coh}}\); hence \(D(f)_{n\text{-coh}}\) is well determined (since it is a full subcategory of \(D(X)\)). To prove existence, we shall need a few lemmas.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 1.1.1.

Let \(i:X\longrightarrow Y\) be a closed immersion of schemes such that \(i_*(\mathcal O_X)\) is pseudo-coherent, and let \(E\in\operatorname{ob}D(X)\). For \(E\) to be \(n\)-pseudo-coherent, it is necessary and sufficient that \(i_*(E)\) be so.

Proof. The "necessity" follows from the general criterion of stability of pseudo-coherence under application of a functor (I 2.13). Conversely, suppose that \(i_*(E)\) is \(n\)-pseudo-coherent, and let us prove that the same holds for \(E\). Since \(i_*(E)\) has cohomology locally bounded above, the same holds for \(E\), hence, the question being local, we may assume that \(E\) is \(m\)-pseudo-coherent for \(m\) large enough. Let us show, by descending induction on \(m\), that \(E\) is \(m\)-pseudo-coherent for every \(m\geq n\). We may assume it already proved that \(E\) is \((n+1)\)-pseudo-coherent. After localizing if necessary, we thus have a distinguished triangle
\[\xymatrix{ &E''\ar[dl]\ar[dr]&\\ E'\ar[rr]&&E }\qquad\text{où }E'\text{ est parfait et }E''{}^i=0\text{ pour }i>n.\]
By (I 2.6), we are reduced to showing that \(E''\) is \(n\)-pseudo-coherent. Now, applying \(i_*\) to the preceding triangle, we obtain a distinguished triangle
\[\xymatrix{ &i_*(E'')\ar[dl]\ar[dr]&\\ i_*(E')\ar[rr]&&i_*(E). }\]

By the first part of the proof, \(i_*(E')\) is pseudo-coherent, hence by virtue of (I 2.6), \(i_*(E'')\) is \(n\)-pseudo-coherent. Since \(i_*(E'')\) is zero in degree \(>n\), this means, by (I 2.10 b)), that \(H^n(i_*(E''))\) is of finite type. Since \(H^n(i_*(E''))\mathrel{\widetilde\longrightarrow}i_*H^n(E'')\), we are reduced to showing, by (I 2.10 b)), that if \(M\) is an \(\mathcal O_X\)-Module such that \(i_*(M)\) is of finite type, then \(M\) is of finite type, which is immediate (and independent of the hypothesis on \(i_*(\mathcal O_X)\)).

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 1.1.2.

The preceding lemma applies in particular to the case where \(i\) is a regular immersion (VII 1.4). Indeed, we have locally, thanks to the Koszul complex, a finite left resolution of \(i_*(\mathcal O_X)\) by free \(\mathcal O_Y\)-Modules of finite type, hence \(i_*(\mathcal O_X)\) is perfect (I 4.8 and 4.14) (hence a fortiori pseudo-coherent).

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 1.1.3.

Consider a cartesian square of schemes

\[\xymatrix{ X\ar[d]_i&X'\ar[l]_f\ar[d]^{i'}\\ Y&Y'\ar[l]^g }\]

where \(i\) is a closed immersion, and let \(E\) be a complex of \(\mathcal O_X\)-Modules. Then the canonical base-change arrow (EGA III 1.4.15.4)

\[g^*i_*(E)\longrightarrow i'_*f^*(E)\]

is an isomorphism.

(ii) Set \(E'=f^*(E)\). If \(g\) is flat and \(i_*(E)\) is \(n\)-pseudo-coherent, then \(i'_*(E')\) is \(n\)-pseudo-coherent. Conversely, if \(g\) is faithfully flat and \(i'_*(E')\) is \(n\)-pseudo-coherent, then \(i_*(E)\) is \(n\)-pseudo-coherent.
Proof. The assertion is verified trivially on the fibers.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 1.1.4.

Consider a commutative diagram of schemes

\[\xymatrix{ X'&X\ar[l]_{i'}\ar[r]^{i''}&X''\\ &Y\ar[ul]^{f'}\ar[ur]_{f''}& }\]

where \(i'\) (resp. \(i''\)) is a closed immersion, and \(f'\) (resp. \(f''\)) a smooth morphism, and let \(E\in\operatorname{ob}D(X)\). We then have:

\[i'_*(E)\ n\text{-pseudo-cohérent}\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad i''_*(E)\ n\text{-pseudo-cohérent}.\]

Proof. Following a trick due to Lichtenbaum, we map \(X\) diagonally into \(X'\times_YX''\), and we are at once reduced to proving the assertion in the case where one of the morphisms \(f'\), \(f''\) is the identity. Changing notation, we thus have a commutative triangle
\[(*)\qquad\xymatrix{ &Y\ar[d]^f&\\ X\ar[ur]^i\ar[r]_j&Z,& }\]

where \(i\) and \(j\) are closed immersions and \(f\) a smooth morphism, and we must show that "\(i_*(E)\) \(n\)-pseudo-coherent" is equivalent to "\(j_*(E)\) \(n\)-pseudo-coherent". The question being local in \(Y\) and in \(Z\) in a neighborhood of a point of \(X\), we may assume that \(X,Y,Z\) are affine and that we have a factorization
\[\xymatrix{ Y\ar[r]^{i'}\ar[d]_f&Z[T_1,\ldots,T_r]\ar[dl]^{f'}\\ Z& }\]

where \(i'\) is a closed immersion, and the oblique arrow the canonical projection. Then (VII 1.10) \(i'\) is a regular immersion, hence (1.1.1) an object \(F\) of \(D(Y)\) is \(n\)-pseudo-coherent if and only if \(i'_*(F)\) is.
We may therefore assume that \(Y=Z[T_1,\ldots,T_r]\), \(f\) being the canonical projection. The diagram \((*)\) then corresponds to a diagram of rings
\[\xymatrix{ &B=C[T_1,\ldots,T_r]\ar[dl]_u&\\ A&C\ar[l]^v\ar[u]_e& }\]

where \(u\) and \(v\) are epimorphisms, and \(e\) the canonical inclusion. For \(1\leq i\leq r\), set \(u(T_i)=x_i\), and let \(z_i\in C\) be such that \(v(z_i)=x_i\). The retraction \(\sigma:B\longrightarrow C\) of \(e\) defined by \(\sigma(T_i)=z_i\) is such that \(v\sigma=u\). In geometric language, it corresponds to a section \(s\) of \(f\) such that \(i=sj\). By (VII 1.10) \(s\) is a regular immersion, so the assertion follows from (1.1.1).

Proof of (1.1) (end). Let \(f:X\longrightarrow Y\) be a morphism locally of finite type. There exists an open covering \((X_\alpha)\) of \(X\) such that each \(X_\alpha\) embeds by a closed immersion into a scheme \(Y_\alpha\) smooth over \(Y\). Setting \(\coprod X_\alpha=X'\), \(\coprod Y_\alpha=Y'\), we thus have a commutative diagram
\[(a')\qquad\xymatrix{ X'\ar[r]^{i'}\ar[d]_{p'}&Y'\ar[dl]^{f'}\\ X\ar[d]_f&\\ Y& }\]

where \(p'\) is a morphism which is a sum of a surjective family of open immersions (we shall say special for short), \(i'\) a closed immersion, and \(f'\) a smooth morphism. We shall define \(D(f)_{n\text{-coh}}\) as the full subcategory of \(D(X)\) consisting of the objects \(E\) such that \(i'_*p'^*(E)\) is \(n\)-pseudo-coherent. Let us first show that this definition makes sense, i.e. that \(D(f)_{n\text{-coh}}\) is independent of the choice of \(i'\), \(p'\), \(f'\). So let
\[(a'')\qquad\xymatrix{ X''\ar[r]^{i''}\ar[d]_{p''}&Y''\ar[dl]^{f''}\\ X\ar[d]_f&\\ Y& }\]
be a commutative diagram analogous to \((a')\) (\(p''\) a special morphism, \(i''\) a closed immersion, \(f''\) a smooth morphism). From \((a')\) and \((a'')\) one deduces a commutative diagram
\[\xymatrix{ X'\times_XX''\ar[r]\ar[d]\ar[dd]&Y'\times_YY''\ar[d]\ar[dd]\\ X'\ar[r]^{i'}\ar[ddr]&Y'\ar[ddr]^{f'}\\ X''\ar[r]^{i''}\ar[dr]&Y''\ar[dr]^{f''}\\ &X\ar[r]_f&Y }\]

where the horizontal arrows, with the exception of \(f\), are closed immersions, and the arrows of the left (resp. right) cartesian square are special morphisms (resp. smooth morphisms). We are thus reduced to proving the following assertion:

blocks.title: \(A\)
blocks.html:
\(A\) :

Let

\[\xymatrix{ X_1\ar[r]^{i_1}\ar[d]_p&Y_1\ar[d]^q\\ X_2\ar[r]^{i_2}&Y_2 }\]

be a commutative square of schemes, where \(p\) is a special morphism, \(q\) a smooth morphism, \(i_1\) and \(i_2\) closed immersions, and let \(E\in\operatorname{ob}D(X_2)\). Then, for \(i_{2*}(E)\) to be \(n\)-pseudo-coherent, it is necessary and sufficient that \(i_{1*}p^*(E)\) be so.

Proof of \(A\). By definition, \(p\) is a sum of a family of open subsets \(U_\alpha\) of \(X_2\) which cover \(X_2\). Each \(U_\alpha\) is induced by an open subset \(V_\alpha\) of \(Y_2\), and the \(V_\alpha\) cover an open neighborhood \(X'_2\) of \(X_2\) in \(Y_2\). We thus have a commutative diagram with cartesian square
\[\xymatrix{ X_1\ar[r]^{i'_1}\ar[d]_p&X'_1\ar[d]^{p'}\\ X_2\ar[r]^{i'_2}\ar@/_1pc/[rr]_{i_2}&X'_2\ar[r]^j&Y_2, }\]

where \(i'_1\) and \(i'_2\) are closed immersions, \(p\) and \(p'\) special morphisms, and \(j\) an open immersion. The condition "\(i_{2*}(E)\) \(n\)-pseudo-coherent" is obviously equivalent to "\(i'_{2*}(E)\) \(n\)-pseudo-coherent". On the other hand, it follows at once from (1.1.3) that the condition "\(i'_{2*}(E)\) \(n\)-pseudo-coherent" is equivalent to "\(i'_{1*}p^*(E)\) \(n\)-pseudo-coherent". Applying then (1.1.4) to the factorizations \((jp')i'_1\) and \(qi_1\) of \(i_2p\), and to \(p^*(E)\in\operatorname{ob}D(X_1)\), we finally conclude that "\(i_{2*}(E)\) \(n\)-pseudo-coherent" is equivalent to "\(i_{1*}p^*(E)\) \(n\)-pseudo-coherent", qed.

We have thus shown that the category \(D(f)_{n\text{-coh}}\) defined above by means of the diagram \((a')\) depends in fact only on \(f\). Moreover, it is trivial from the definition that it does satisfy conditions (i) to (iii) of (1.1). This therefore completes the proof of (1.1).

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 1.2.

Let \(f:X\longrightarrow Y\) be a morphism locally of finite type, \(E\in\operatorname{ob}D(X)\), \(n\in\mathbb Z\). We shall say that \(E\) is \(n\)-pseudo-coherent relative to \(f\) if \(E\in\operatorname{ob}D(f)_{n\text{-coh}}\) (cf. (1.1)). We shall say that \(E\) is pseudo-coherent (or also \((-\infty)\)-pseudo-coherent) relative to \(f\) if \(E\) is \(n\)-pseudo-coherent relative to \(f\) for every \(n\in\mathbb Z\). We shall denote by \(D(f)_{\mathrm{coh}}\) the full subcategory of \(D(X)\) consisting of the objects that are pseudo-coherent relative to \(f\).

We shall say that \(f\) is \(n\)-pseudo-coherent (resp. pseudo-coherent) if \(\mathcal O_X\) is \(n\)-pseudo-coherent (resp. pseudo-coherent) relative to \(f\). We shall sometimes say, when no confusion can arise, "\(n\)-pseudo-coherent (resp. pseudo-coherent) relative to \(Y\)" instead of "relative to \(f\)", and we shall also write \(D(X)_{Y\text{-}n\text{-coh}}\) (resp. \(D(X)_{Y\text{-coh}}\)) instead of \(D(f)_{n\text{-coh}}\) (resp. \(D(f)_{\mathrm{coh}}\)). Finally, for \(*=+,-,b\), we shall set

\[D^*(f)_{n\text{-coh}}=D^*(X)\cap D(f)_{n\text{-coh}},\] \[D^*(f)_{\mathrm{coh}}=D^*(X)\cap D(f)_{\mathrm{coh}}.\]

blocks.title: Remarks
blocks.html:
Remarks 1.2.1.

a) The categories \(D(f)_{n\text{-coh}}\), \(D(f)_{\mathrm{coh}}\) are not in general contained in \(D^-(X)\). This is however the case if \(X\) is quasi-compact, for an object of \(D(X)\) with cohomology locally bounded above then has cohomology bounded above.

b) The objects of \(D(f)_{\mathrm{coh}}\) have quasi-coherent cohomology. Indeed, let \(E\in\operatorname{ob}D(f)_{\mathrm{coh}}\); the question being local, we may assume that \(f\) is a closed immersion, then \(f_*(E)\) is pseudo-coherent, hence has quasi-coherent cohomology, and one concludes by the fact that an \(\mathcal O_X\)-Module \(M\) is quasi-coherent if and only if \(f_*(M)\) is.

c) When dealing with an \(\mathcal O_X\)-Module, we shall use the terminology "of finite presentation rel. to \(f\)" in preference to "\((-1)\)-pseudo-coherent rel. to \(f\)". For an \(\mathcal O_X\)-Module \(E\) to be of finite presentation rel. to \(f\), it is necessary and sufficient, in view of (I 2.9), that there exist locally a closed immersion \(i:X\longrightarrow X'\), with \(X'\) smooth over \(Y\), such that \(i_*(E)\) is of finite presentation (in the ordinary sense). In particular, to say that \(f\) is
\((-1)\)-pseudo-coherent amounts to saying that \(f\) is locally of finite presentation in the sense of (EGA IV 1.4).

blocks.title: Examples
blocks.html:
Examples 1.3.

A smooth morphism, a regular immersion (1.1.2) are pseudo-coherent morphisms. The same holds for an arbitrary morphism \(f:X\longrightarrow Y\) locally of finite type if \(Y\) is locally noetherian.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 1.4.

Let \(f:X\longrightarrow Y\) be a morphism locally of finite type, and let

\[\xymatrix{ &G\ar[dl]\ar[dr]&\\ E\ar[rr]&&F }\]

be a distinguished triangle of \(D(X)\). We then have the following table, where \(n\in\mathbb Z\cup\{-\infty\}\) indicates a degree of pseudo-coherence with respect to \(f\), and each row represents an implication whose target is underlined:

\[\begin{array}{c|ccc} &E&F&G\\ \hline &n+1&n&\underline n\\ &n&\underline n&n\\ &\underline n&n&n-1 \end{array}\]

In particular, \(D(f)_{\mathrm{coh}}\) is a triangulated subcategory of \(D(X)\).
Proof. By virtue of properties (i) to (iii) of (1.1), we may assume that \(f\) is a closed immersion. We then have a distinguished triangle
\[\xymatrix{ &f_*(G)\ar[dl]\ar[dr]&\\ f_*(E)\ar[rr]&&f_*(F), }\]
and the assertion follows from (I 2.6).

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 1.5.

Let \(X\) and \(Y\) be two \(S\)-schemes. We shall say that \(X\) and \(Y\) are tor-independent over \(S\) if we have

\[\operatorname{Tor}_i^S(\mathcal O_X,\mathcal O_Y)=0\qquad\text{pour }i\neq0,\]

the \(\operatorname{Tor}_i^S\) being the local hyper-tor defined in (EGA III 6.5).
This is a condition of local nature; if \(X,Y,S\) are affine with respective rings \(B,C,A\), it means that the canonical arrow
\[B\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}_A}C\longrightarrow B\otimes_A C\]
is an isomorphism. It is therefore satisfied in particular if \(X\) or \(Y\) is flat over \(S\).

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 1.5.1.

Consider cartesian squares of schemes

\[\xymatrix{ X&X'\ar[l]&X''\ar[l]\\ Y\ar[u]&Y'\ar[l]\ar[u]&Y''\ar[l]\ar[u] }\]

If \(X\) and \(Y'\) are tor-independent over \(Y\), and if \(X'\) and \(Y''\) are tor-independent over \(Y'\), then \(X\) and \(Y''\) are tor-independent over \(Y\).
Proof. An easy exercise on the associativity of the tensor product.
Notation 1.6.
Let \(X\) and \(Y\) be two \(S\)-schemes, \(E\in\operatorname{ob}D^-(X)\), \(F\in\operatorname{ob}D^-(Y)\). We shall set
\[E\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}_S}F =L\operatorname{pr}_1^*(E)\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}}L\operatorname{pr}_2^*(F),\]
where \(\operatorname{pr}_1,\operatorname{pr}_2\) are the two projections of \(X\times_SY\).

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 1.7.

Let \(S\) be a scheme, and for \(i=1,2\), let \(f_i:X_i\longrightarrow Y_i\) be a closed \(S\)-immersion. We assume that \(X_1\) and \(X_2\), as well as \(Y_1\) and \(Y_2\), are tor-independent over \(S\). Then, for \(E_i\in\operatorname{ob}D^-(X_i)_{\mathrm{qcoh}}\)(1), there exists a canonical isomorphism

\[f_{1*}(E_1)\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}_S}f_{2*}(E_2) \mathrel{\widetilde\longrightarrow}(f_1\times_Sf_2)_*\bigl(E_1\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}_S}E_2\bigr).\]
Proof. Set \(f_1\times_Sf_2=f\). One easily defines, by adjunction, a canonical arrow
\[f_{1*}(E_1)\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}_S}f_{2*}(E_2) \longrightarrow f_*\bigl(E_1\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}_S}E_2\bigr).\]

Let us show that it is an isomorphism. The question being local, we may assume \(S,X_i,Y_i\) affine with respective rings \(C,A_i,B_i\). By dévissage, we reduce to \(E_i=\mathcal O_{X_i}\). We must then check that
\[A_1\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}_{B_1}}B \mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}_{B_2}}A_2\longrightarrow A\]
is an isomorphism (we have set \(A=A_1\otimes_CA_2\), \(B=B_1\otimes_CB_2\)). But, in view of the tor-independence hypotheses, this follows at once from the associativity of the tensor product.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 1.8.

Let \(S\) be a scheme, and, for \(i=1,2\), let \(f_i:X_i\longrightarrow Y_i\) be an \(S\)-morphism locally of finite type. We assume \(X_1\) and \(X_2\), as well as \(Y_1\) and \(Y_2\), tor-independent over \(S\). Let \([a_i,b_i]\) be an interval of \(\mathbb Z\), and \(E_i\in\operatorname{ob}D^-(X_i)_{\mathrm{qcoh}}\) a complex which is \(a_i\)-pseudo-coherent rel. to \(f_i\) and acyclic in degree \(>b_i\). Then \(E_1\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}_S}E_2\) is \(a\)-pseudo-coherent rel. to \(f\) and acyclic in degree \(>b\), with

\[f=f_1\times_Sf_2,\qquad a=\operatorname{Sup}(a_1+b_2,a_2+b_1),\qquad b=b_1+b_2.\]
Proof. The question being local on \(X_1\times_SX_2\), we may assume that \(f_i\) factors as
\[X_i\xrightarrow{f'_i}Y'_i\xrightarrow{p_i}Y_i,\]

where \(f'_i\) is a closed immersion and \(p_i\) a smooth morphism. Setting \(f'=f'_1\times_Sf'_2\), we must show that \(f'_*(E_1\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}_S}E_2)\) is \(a\)-pseudo-coherent and acyclic in degree \(>b\). By (1.5.1), \(Y'_1\) and \(Y'_2\) are tor-independent over \(S\). After a change of notation, we may therefore assume that \(f_i=f'_i\), in other words that \(f_i\) is a closed immersion. By hypothesis, \(f_{i*}(E_i)\) is \(a_i\)-pseudo-coherent and acyclic in degree \(>b_i\), hence (I 2.16) \(f_{1*}(E_1)\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}_S}f_{2*}(E_2)\) is \(a\)-pseudo-coherent and acyclic in degree \(>b\). We conclude thanks to (1.7).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 1.9.

Under the hypotheses of (1.8), the functor

\[\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}_S}:D^-(X_1)\times D^-(X_2)\longrightarrow D^-(X)\]

induces a functor

\[\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}_S}:D^-(f_1)_{\mathrm{coh}}\times D^-(f_2)_{\mathrm{coh}} \longrightarrow D^-(f)_{\mathrm{coh}}\qquad\text{(cf. (1.2)).}\]

In particular, if \(f_1\) and \(f_2\) are pseudo-coherent (1.2), the same holds for \(f\).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 1.10.

Let

\[\xymatrix{ X\ar[d]_f&X'\ar[l]_g\ar[d]^{f'}\\ Y&Y'\ar[l]^h }\]

be a cartesian square, with \(f\) locally of finite type. Then:

a) Suppose \(X\) and \(Y'\) tor-independent over \(Y\) (1.5). For \(n\in\mathbb Z\cup\{-\infty\}\), the functor \(Lg^*:D^-(X)\longrightarrow D^-(X')\) induces a functor

\[Lg^*:D^-(f)_{n\text{-coh}}\longrightarrow D^-(f')_{n\text{-coh}}.\]

In particular, if \(f\) is \(n\)-pseudo-coherent (1.2), the same holds for \(f'\).

b) Suppose \(h\) faithfully flat, let \(n\in\mathbb Z\cup\{-\infty\}\) and let \(E\in\operatorname{ob}D^-(X)\) be a complex with quasi-coherent cohomology. For \(E\) to be \(n\)-pseudo-coherent rel. to \(f\), it is necessary and sufficient that \(g^*(E)\) be \(n\)-pseudo-coherent rel. to \(f'\). In particular, for \(f\) to be \(n\)-pseudo-coherent, it is necessary and sufficient that \(f'\) be so.

Proof. In view of (1.2.1 b)), all the assertions of (1.9) and (1.10) follow trivially from (1.8), except the "sufficiency" part of (1.10 b)). For the latter, since the question is local on \(X\), we may assume that \(f\) factors as a closed immersion followed by a smooth morphism. We are then reduced by (1.1.3) to the case where \(f\) (resp. \(f'\)) is the identity of \(X\) (resp. \(X'\)). The assertion then follows, by a standard dévissage (cf. I 2.16.2), from the fact that a quasi-coherent sheaf \(L\) on \(X\) is of finite type if and only if \(g^*L\) is of finite type.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 1.11.

Let

\[\xymatrix{ X\ar[r]^f\ar[dr]_h&Y\ar[d]^g\\ &Z }\]

be a commutative triangle of morphisms locally of finite type, let \(E\in\operatorname{ob}D(X)\), \(F\in\operatorname{ob}D(Y)\), and let \([a,b]\), \([c,d]\) be intervals of \(\mathbb Z\).

a) If \(E\) is \(a\)-pseudo-coherent rel. to \(f\) and acyclic in degree \(>b\) and if \(F\) is \(c\)-pseudo-coherent rel. to \(g\) and acyclic in degree \(>d\), then \(E\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}_Y}F\) (cf. (1.6)) is \(m\)-pseudo-coherent rel. to \(h\) and acyclic in degree \(>n\), where

\[m=\operatorname{Sup}(a+d,b+c),\qquad n=b+d.\]

b) If \(E\) is \(a\)-pseudo-coherent rel. to \(h\) and acyclic in degree \(>b\), and if \(g\) is \((a+1-b)\)-pseudo-coherent, then \(E\) is \(a\)-pseudo-coherent rel. to \(f\).
Proof. The question being local on \(X\), we may assume that we have a commutative diagram with cartesian square
\[\xymatrix{ &&\mathbf E_Z^{r+s}\ar[dl]\ar[dr]&\\ &\mathbf E_Y^r\ar[ur]^j\ar[dr]_p&&\mathbf E_Z^s\ar[dl]^k\ar[dr]^q&\\ X\ar[ur]^i\ar[rr]_f&&Y\ar[rr]_g&&Z, }\]

where \(i,j,k\) are closed immersions, and \(p,q\) the canonical projections of the standard affine spaces. If \(F\) is \(c\)-pseudo-coherent rel. to \(Z\), then \(k_*(F)\) is \(c\)-pseudo-coherent by (1.1 (iii)), and \(j_*(p^*(F))\) is \(c\)-pseudo-coherent by (1.1.3). Similarly, if \(g\) is \(x\)-pseudo-coherent, then \(j_*(\mathcal O_{\mathbf E_Y^r})\) is \(x\)-pseudo-coherent. By the definition of relative pseudo-coherence (1.2), we are thus reduced to proving a) and b) when \(f\) and \(g\) are closed immersions.

Proof of a). By (1.1 (iii)), we must show the following: suppose \(f_*(E)\) is \(a\)-pseudo-coherent and acyclic in degree \(>b\), and \(g_*(F)\) is \(c\)-pseudo-coherent and acyclic in degree \(>d\); then \(h_*(E\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}_Y}F)\) is \(m\)-pseudo-coherent and acyclic in degree \(>n\). Now we have a canonical isomorphism
\[f_*(E)\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}}F \mathrel{\widetilde\longrightarrow}f_*\bigl(E\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}_Y}F\bigr)\]

(a special case of the isomorphism \((*)\) of the proof of (1.8)). Since the functor \(g_*(\,\cdot\,\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}}F)\) transforms locally free sheaves of finite type into \(c\)-pseudo-coherent complexes, and complexes acyclic in degree \(>0\) into complexes acyclic in degree \(>d\), the assertion thus follows from (I 2.13).

Proof of b). We must prove the following assertion: suppose \(h_*(E)\) is \(a\)-pseudo-coherent and acyclic in degree \(>b\), and \(g_*(\mathcal O_Y)\) is \((a+1-b)\)-pseudo-coherent; then \(f_*(E)\) is \(a\)-pseudo-coherent. Since the assertion concerns \(f_*(E)\), we may assume \(X=Y\) and \(f=\operatorname{Id}_X\). We then proceed as in the proof of (1.1.1), proving by descending induction that \(E\) is pseudo-coherent in degree \(\geq a\). We assume it already proved that \(E\) is \((a+1)\)-pseudo-coherent, whence (I 2.10), after localizing if necessary, a distinguished triangle
\[\xymatrix{ &E''\ar[dl]\ar[dr]&\\ E'\ar[rr]&&E, }\]

where \(E'^i\) is locally free for \(i\in[a+1,b]\), \(E'^i=0\) for \(i\notin[a+1,b]\), and \(E''{}^i=0\) for \(i>a\). The hypothesis that \(g_*(\mathcal O_Y)\) is \((a+1-b)\)-pseudo-coherent then implies (I 2.11 a)) that \(g_*(E')\) is \((a+1)\)-pseudo-coherent. Since \(g_*(E)\) is \(a\)-pseudo-coherent, it follows from (I 2.5) that \(g_*(E'')\) is \(a\)-pseudo-coherent. We conclude thanks to (I 2.10 b)) and to the fact that an \(\mathcal O_Y\)-Module \(M\) is of finite type if and only if \(g_*(M)\) is.
One deduces trivially from (1.11) the following corollaries:

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 1.12.

In the situation of (1.11), if \(g\) is pseudo-coherent, we have, for \(r\in\mathbb Z\cup\{-\infty\}\),

\[D^-(f)_{r\text{-coh}}=D^-(h)_{r\text{-coh}}\ ;\]

in particular (taking \(X=Y\)), the notions of \(r\)-pseudo-coherence on \(Y\), "in the absolute sense" or rel. to \(g\), coincide.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 1.13.

In the situation of (1.11), if \(f\) is pseudo-coherent, the functor

\[\mathcal O_X\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}_Y}\,\cdot\,=Lf^*:D^-(Y)\longrightarrow D^-(X)\]

induces, for \(r\in\mathbb Z\cup\{-\infty\}\), a functor

\[Lf^*:D^-(g)_{r\text{-coh}}\longrightarrow D^-(h)_{r\text{-coh}}.\]

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 1.14.

In the situation of (1.11), if \(f\) is \(r\)-pseudo-coherent and \(g\) is \(s\)-pseudo-coherent (\(r,s\in\mathbb Z\cup\{-\infty\}\)), then \(h\) is \(\operatorname{Sup}(r,s)\)-pseudo-coherent. If \(h\) is \(r\)-pseudo-coherent and \(g\) is \((r+1)\)-pseudo-coherent, \(f\) is \(r\)-pseudo-coherent.

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 1.14.1.

In particular, for \(r=s=-1\), we recover (cf. (1.2.1 c))) assertions (ii) and \(v\) of (EGA IV 1.4.3).

footnotes.html:
\(D(X)\) = derived category of that of \(\mathcal O_X\)-Modules.
(2) \(D(Y)_{n\text{-coh}}\) = full subcategory of \(D(Y)\) consisting of the \(n\)-pseudo-coherent objects (I 2.15).
\(D(X_i)_{\mathrm{qcoh}}\) denotes the full subcategory of \(D(X_i)\) consisting of the complexes with quasi-coherent cohomology.

blocks.html: 2. The finiteness theorem

blocks.title: Conjecture
blocks.html:
Conjecture 2.1.

Let \(S\) be a scheme, \(f:X\longrightarrow Y\) a proper \(S\)-morphism of schemes locally of finite type over \(S\), \(E\in\operatorname{ob}D^+(X)\). Then if \(E\) is pseudo-coherent rel. to \(S\) (1.2), the same holds for \(Rf_*(E)\).

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 2.2.

The preceding conjecture is true in the following cases:

(i) \(S\) is locally noetherian;

(ii) \(f\) is projective.

Proof in case (i). \(S\) being locally noetherian, the projection of \(X\) (resp. \(Y\)) onto \(S\) is a pseudo-coherent morphism (1.3), and the notions of pseudo-coherence on \(X\) (resp. \(Y\)) and relative to \(S\) coincide (1.12). On the other hand, since \(X\) (resp. \(Y\)) is locally noetherian, hence \(\mathcal O_X\) (resp. \(\mathcal O_Y\)) coherent, an object \(M\) of \(D(X)\) (resp. \(D(Y)\)) is pseudo-coherent if and only if (I 3.5) the \(H^i(M)\) are coherent and \(M\) is locally in \(D^-\). The theorem therefore follows from the following one:

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 2.2.1.

Let \(f:X\longrightarrow Y\) be a proper morphism of locally noetherian schemes, and let \(E\in\operatorname{ob}D^+(X)\). If the \(H^i(E)\) are coherent, the same holds for the \(R^if_*(E)\). If moreover \(E\) is locally in \(D^-\), the same holds for \(Rf_*(E)\).
Proof. The first assertion follows from the spectral sequence
\[(*)\qquad R^pf_*\bigl(H^q(E)\bigr)\Longrightarrow R^{p+q}f_*(E)\]

and from the finiteness theorem (EGA III 3.2.1). For the second, we may assume \(Y\) affine; \(X\) is then quasi-compact and separated; suppose it covered by \(r\) affine open subsets. It is known (EGA III 1.4.12) that, under these conditions, we have \(R^if_*(M)=0\) for every quasi-coherent \(\mathcal O_X\)-Module \(M\) and every \(i>r\). If \(E\) is locally in \(D^-\), then \(E\) is in \(D^-\) since \(X\) is quasi-compact, say \(H^i(E)=0\) for \(i>N\). From the spectral sequence \((*)\) one then deduces that \(R^if_*(E)=0\) for \(i>N+r\), whence the assertion.
Proof in case (ii). The question being local on \(Y\), we may assume \(S\) and \(Y\) affine. We then have a commutative diagram with cartesian square
\[\xymatrix{ &&\mathbf P_{Y'}^r\ar[dr]^{p'}&\\ &\mathbf P_Y^r\ar[ur]^j\ar[dr]_p&&Y'\ar[dr]^{g'}&\\ X\ar[ur]^i\ar[rr]_f&&Y\ar[ur]^{j'}\ar[rr]_g&&S, }\]

where \(i,j,j'\) are closed immersions, \(g'\) a smooth affine morphism, and \(p,p'\) the projection of the standard projective space. Suppose \(E\) pseudo-coherent rel. to \(S\). By (1.1 (iii)), this means that \((ji)_*(E)\) is pseudo-coherent. We must show that \(Rf_*(E)\) is pseudo-coherent rel. to \(S\), i.e. (1.1 (iii)) that \(j'_*Rf_*(E)\) is pseudo-coherent. But we have
\[j'_*Rf_*(E)\mathrel{\widetilde\longrightarrow}Rp'_*(ji)_*(E),\]
the assertion will therefore be a consequence of the following more precise result:

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 2.2.2.

Let \(Y\) be an affine scheme, \(f:X=\mathbf P_Y^r\longrightarrow Y\) the projection of a standard projective space, \(E\in\operatorname{ob}D^b(X)_{\mathrm{coh}}\), \(a\in\mathbb Z\). Then \(Rf_*(E)\) is pseudo-coherent. Moreover, if \(E\) is acyclic in degree \(>a\), then \(Rf_*(E)\) is acyclic in degree \(>a+r\), and there exists an integer \(N\) such that, for every \(n\geq N\), \(Rf_*(E(n))\) (where \(E(n)=E\otimes\mathcal O_X(n)\)) is acyclic in degree \(>a\).

Proof. Since \(Y\) is affine, \(X\) is quasi-compact and separated, hence (II 2.2.9 a)) \(E\) is isomorphic, in \(D(X)\), to a complex \(F\) with degrees bounded above such that, for every \(i\), \(F^i\in\operatorname{ob}\underline L(X)\), i.e. (loc. cit.) is a finite direct sum of sheaves of the type \(\mathcal O_X(-n)\) (\(n>0\)). Moreover, if \(E\) is acyclic in degree \(>a\), one can choose \(F\) in such a way that \(F^i=0\) for \(i>a\): this follows formally from the preceding assertion, in view of (I 2.10 a) and II 2.2.8 a)). Replacing \(E\) by \(F\) if necessary, we may therefore assume that \(E^i\in\operatorname{ob}\underline L(X)\) for every \(i\), and \(E^i=0\) for \(i>a\). Since \(X\) is covered by \((r+1)\) affine open subsets, we have, by (EGA III 1.4.10) and the hypercohomology spectral sequence, \(R^if_*(E)=0\) for \(i>a+r\). Let \(t\) be an integer \(\leq a\), and set
\[E'=(0\longrightarrow E^t\longrightarrow\cdots\longrightarrow E^a\longrightarrow0).\]
We have an exact sequence
\[0\longrightarrow E'\longrightarrow E\longrightarrow E''\longrightarrow0,\]
where \(E''\) satisfies the same conditions as \(E\), but with \(a\) replaced by \(t-1\). Applying \(Rf_*\), we obtain a distinguished triangle
\[(*)\qquad\xymatrix{ &Rf_*(E'')\ar[dl]\ar[dr]&\\ Rf_*(E')\ar[rr]&&Rf_*(E). }\]

Since \(t\) can be chosen arbitrarily large negative, it suffices, for the first assertion, to show that \(Rf_*(E)\) is \((t+r)\)-pseudo-coherent. Now \(Rf_*(E'')\) is acyclic in degree \(\geq t+r\), hence \((t+r)\)-pseudo-coherent. We shall therefore be done, by virtue of (I 2.6), if we prove that \(Rf_*(E')\) is perfect. For this, we reduce by dévissage (I 4.10) to the case where \(E'\) is concentrated in degree 0 and equal to \(\mathcal O_X(-n)\), \(n>0\). Then, by (EGA III 2.1.16), \(R^rf_*(\mathcal O_X(-n))\) is locally free of finite type, and we have
\[Rf_*(\mathcal O_X(-n))\mathrel{\widetilde\longrightarrow} R^rf_*(\mathcal O_X(-n))[-r].\]

Hence \(Rf_*(\mathcal O_X(-n))\) is perfect, and the first assertion of (2.2.2) follows. We have already seen that \(Rf_*(E)\) is acyclic in degree \(>a+r\). It remains to prove the last assertion. For \(t<-r+a\), \(Rf_*(E'')\) is acyclic in degree \(>a\), so we are reduced, in view of the triangle \((*)\), to carrying out the proof for \(E'\) instead of \(E\). Since \(E'\) has bounded degrees and components which are objects of \(\underline L(X)\), there exists an integer \(N\) such that, for \(n\geq N\), the components of \(E'(n)\) are finite direct sums of sheaves of the type \(\mathcal O_X(p)\) where \(p\geq0\). Then, by (EGA III 2.1.16) and the hypercohomology spectral sequence, \(Rf_*(E'(n))\) is acyclic in degree \(>a\) for every \(n\geq N\), which completes the proof of (2.2.2), and consequently of (2.2).
We have a "classical" complement to (2.2 (ii)), which we shall give for later reference:

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 2.3. (EGA III 2.2.1).

Let \(S\) be a quasi-compact scheme, \(f:X\longrightarrow Y\) a projective \(S\)-morphism of schemes of finite type over \(S\), \(L=i^*\mathcal O_P(1)\) the invertible sheaf defined by an embedding \(i:X\longrightarrow P=\mathbf P(F)\), where \(F\) is a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal O_Y\)-Module of finite type. For \(M\in\operatorname{ob}D(X)\) and \(n\in\mathbb Z\), set \(M(n)=M\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}}L^{\otimes n}\). Then, if \(E\in\operatorname{ob}D^+(X)\) is pseudo-coherent rel. to \(S\) and acyclic in degree \(>a\) (\(a\in\mathbb Z\)), there exists an integer \(N\) such that, for every \(n\geq N\), \(Rf_*E(n)\) satisfies the same conditions.

Proof. One reduces trivially, as in the proof of (2.2 (ii)), to the situation of (2.2.2): \(Y=S\), \(f:X=\mathbf P_Y^r\longrightarrow Y\) the canonical projection, \(L=\mathcal O_Y(1)\) the canonical sheaf, \(E\in\operatorname{ob}D^b(X)\) a pseudo-coherent complex acyclic in degree \(>a\), and one concludes thanks to (2.2.2).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 2.4.

Let \(S\) be a quasi-compact scheme, and \(f:X\longrightarrow Y\) a proper \(S\)-morphism of schemes of finite type over \(S\). If \(S\) is noetherian or if \(f\) is projective, the functor \(Rf_*:D^+(X)\longrightarrow D^+(Y)\) induces a functor

\[Rf_*:D^b(X)_{S\text{-coh}}\longrightarrow D^b(Y)_{S\text{-coh}}.\]

Proof. A consequence of (2.2) and of the fact that there then exists (EGA III 1.4.12 and IV 1.7.21) an integer \(N\) such that \(R^if_*(E)=0\) for every \(i>N\) and every quasi-coherent \(\mathcal O_X\)-Module \(E\).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 2.5.

Let \(f:X\longrightarrow Y\) be a proper and pseudo-coherent morphism (1.2), and let \(E\in\operatorname{ob}D^+(X)\). Suppose \(Y\) locally noetherian or \(f\) projective. Then, if \(E\) is pseudo-coherent (in the absolute sense), the same holds for \(Rf_*(E)\). If \(Y\) is quasi-compact, the functor \(Rf_*\) induces a functor

\[Rf_*:D^b(X)_{\mathrm{coh}}\longrightarrow D^b(Y)_{\mathrm{coh}}.\]
Proof. A consequence of (2.2), (2.5) and (1.12).
Scholium 2.6.

To prove conjecture (2.1) in the general case, it will no doubt be necessary to bring new techniques into play. One may hope that these will one day help to prove the analogous conjecture in Analytic Geometry, for a proper morphism of analytic spaces over a topos locally ringed in suitably topologized \(\mathbb C\)-algebras, a conjecture which for the moment is "proved" only over a complex analytic space (theorem of Grauert [1]).(1)

footnotes.html: (added in April 1971) see an article in preparation by R. Kiehl and J.L. Verdier, where the finiteness theorem 1 is proved by different, simpler techniques, and under more general hypotheses.

blocks.html: 3. Relative finite tor-dimension

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 3.1.

Let \(f:(X,\mathcal O_X)\longrightarrow(Y,\mathcal O_Y)\) be a morphism of ringed topoi, \(E\in\operatorname{ob}D^-(X)\), \([m,n]\) an interval of \(\mathbb Z\). We say that \(E\) has flat amplitude relative to \(f\) (or \(Y\), if no confusion is possible) contained in \([m,n]\), and we write

\[\operatorname{tor.amp}_f(E)\subset[m,n] \qquad\bigl(\text{ou }\operatorname{tor.amp}_Y(E)\subset[m,n]\bigr)\]

if \(E\), considered as a complex of \(f^{-1}(\mathcal O_Y)\)-Modules, has flat amplitude contained in \([m,n]\) (I 5.2). We say that \(f\) has flat amplitude contained in \([-a,0]\) (\(a\geq0\)), or is of tor-dimension \(\leq a\), if \(\mathcal O_X\) has flat amplitude rel. to \(f\), contained in \([-a,0]\). We say that \(E\) has finite flat amplitude rel. to \(f\) if there exists an interval \([m,n]\) such that we have \(\operatorname{tor.amp}_f(E)\subset[m,n]\).
Notation 3.2.

We shall denote by \(D(f)_{\mathrm{torf}}\), or \(D(X)_{f\text{-torf}}\), or \(D(X)_{Y\text{-torf}}\) if no confusion is possible, the full subcategory of \(D^-(X)\) consisting of the complexes of finite flat amplitude relative to \(f\). By virtue of (I 5.3), it is a triangulated subcategory of \(D^b(X)\).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 3.3.

The data being those of (3.1), suppose that \(X\) has enough points (SGA 4 XVII IV 6.4). The following conditions are then equivalent:

(i) \(\operatorname{tor.amp}_f(E)\subset[m,n]\);

(ii) for every point \(x\) of \(X\), we have

\[\operatorname{tor.amp}_y(E_x)\subset[m,n],\]

where \(y\) is the image point of \(x\) under \(f\), ringed by \(\mathcal O_{Y,y}\);

(iii) for every object \(V\) of \(Y\) and every \(\mathcal O_V\)-Module \(M\), we have

\[H^i\bigl((E|f^{-1}(V))\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}_V}M\bigr)=0 \quad\text{pour }i\notin[m,n],\]

where \((E|f^{-1}(V))\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}_V}M=(E|f^{-1}(V))\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}}Lf^*(M)\);

(iv) the same condition as (iii), but with \(M\) the cokernel of a homomorphism

\[\mathcal O_V^q\longrightarrow\mathcal O_V^p,\qquad\text{où }p,q\in\mathbb N.\]

Proof. The equivalence (i) \(\Longleftrightarrow\) (ii) follows from (I 5.6.2), and the implications (ii) \(\Longrightarrow\) (iii) \(\Longrightarrow\) (iv) are trivial. Let us prove (iv) \(\Longrightarrow\) (ii). Let \(x\) be a point of \(X\), \(y=f(x)\); we must show that we have
\[(*)\qquad H^i\bigl(E_x\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}_{\mathcal O_{Y,y}}}M\bigr)=0\]

for every \(\mathcal O_{Y,y}\)-module \(M\) and every \(i\notin[m,n]\). Since every \(\mathcal O_{Y,y}\)-module is a filtered inductive limit of modules of finite presentation, we may confine ourselves to checking the preceding relation for \(M\) of finite presentation. Now, if \(M\) is an \(\mathcal O_{Y,y}\)-module of finite presentation, the same argument as for ordinary ringed spaces (EGA 0I 5.3.9) shows that there exists an \(y\)-pointed object \(V\) of \(Y\) and an \(\mathcal O_V\)-Module \(P\), the cokernel of a homomorphism \(\mathcal O_V^q\longrightarrow\mathcal O_V^p\) (\(p,q\in\mathbb N\)), such that we have \(P_y\mathrel{\widetilde\longrightarrow}M\). We then have
\[\bigl((E|f^{-1}(V))\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}_V}P\bigr)_x \mathrel{\widetilde\longrightarrow}E_x\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}_{\mathcal O_{Y,y}}}M,\]
by the commutation of the tensor product with passage to stalks, and hypothesis (iv) implies \((*)\), qed.

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 3.3.1.

The editor does not know whether the equivalences (i) \(\Longleftrightarrow\) (iii) \(\Longleftrightarrow\) (iv) are true without the hypothesis that \(X\) has enough points.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 3.4.

Let

\[\xymatrix{ X\ar[r]^f\ar[dr]_h&Y\ar[d]^g\\ &Z }\]

be a commutative triangle of ringed topoi, where we assume that \(X\) has enough points, and let \(E\in\operatorname{ob}D(X)\), \(F\in\operatorname{ob}D(Y)\), \([a,b]\), \([c,d]\) be intervals of \(\mathbb Z\). We then have:

\[\operatorname{tor.amp}_f(E)\subset[a,b]\ \text{ et }\ \operatorname{tor.amp}_g(F)\subset[c,d]\] \[\Longrightarrow\quad \operatorname{tor.amp}_h\bigl(E\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}_Y}F\bigr) \subset[a+c,b+d].\]
Proof. By (3.3), we must show that the relations on the left imply
\[H^i\Bigl(\bigl((E\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}_Y}F)|h^{-1}(Z')\bigr) \mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}_{Z'}}M\Bigr)=0\]
for every \(i\notin[a+c,b+d]\) and every \(\mathcal O_{Z'}\)-Module \(M\), where \(Z'\) is an object of \(Z\). Now, if we set \(Y'=g^{-1}(Z')\), \(X'=f^{-1}(Y')=h^{-1}(Z')\), we have

\[\bigl((E\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}_Y}F)|X'\bigr) \mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}_{Z'}}M \mathrel{\widetilde\longrightarrow} (E|X')\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}_{Y'}} \Bigl((F|Y')\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}_{Z'}}M\Bigr),\]
and the assertion follows at once from the hypotheses.
In particular:

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 3.4.1.

a) If \(g\) is of tor-dimension \(\leq n\), we have

\[\operatorname{tor.amp}_f(E)\subset[a,b] \Longrightarrow\operatorname{tor.amp}_h(E)\subset[a-n,b]\ ;\]

b) if \(f\) is of tor-dimension \(\leq m\), we have

\[\operatorname{tor.amp}_g(F)\subset[c,d] \Longrightarrow\operatorname{tor.amp}_h(Lf^*(F))\subset[c-m,d].\]

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 3.4.2.

Let \(f:X\longrightarrow Y\) be a morphism of finite tor-dimension and \(E\in\operatorname{ob}D(X)\). Then, by (3.4.1 a)), if \(E\) has finite flat amplitude (in the absolute sense), \(E\) has finite flat amplitude rel. to \(f\). But the converse is false, even if \(f\) is flat, as is shown by the example of a singularity of a scheme over a field.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 3.5.

Let \(S\) be a scheme, and, for \(i=1,2\), let \(f_i:X_i\longrightarrow Y_i\) be an \(S\)-morphism, \(E_i\in\operatorname{ob}D^-(X_i)\), \([a_i,b_i]\) an interval of \(\mathbb Z\). Suppose \(X_1\) and \(X_2\), as well as \(Y_1\) and \(Y_2\), tor-independent over \(S\) (1.5). Set

\[a=a_1+a_2,\qquad b=b_1+b_2,\qquad f=f_1\times_Sf_2:X\longrightarrow Y.\]

Then:

a) If we have \(\operatorname{tor.amp}_{f_1}(E_1)\subset[a_1,b_1]\) and if \(E_2\) is acyclic outside \([a_2,b_2]\), then \(E_1\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}_S}E_2\) (cf. (1.6)) is acyclic outside \([a,b]\).

b) If we have \(\operatorname{tor.amp}_{f_i}(E_i)\subset[a_i,b_i]\) for \(i=1,2\), we have

\[\operatorname{tor.amp}_f\bigl(E_1\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}_S}E_2\bigr)\subset[a,b].\]

Proof. By (3.3), it suffices to make the verification "on the stalks". Let \(s\) be a point of \(S\), \(x\) a point of \(X\) above \(s\), \(y=f(x)\), and let \(x_i\) (resp. \(y_i\)) be the canonical projection of \(x\) (resp. \(y\)) onto \(X_i\) (resp. \(Y_i\)). Set
\[(E_i)_{x_i}=M_i,\quad \mathcal O_{X_i,x_i}=A_i,\quad \mathcal O_{Y_i,y_i}=B_i,\quad\mathcal O_{S,s}=C,\quad \mathcal O_{X,x}=A=A_1\otimes_CA_2,\quad \mathcal O_{Y,y}=B=B_1\otimes_CB_2.\]
We have

\[(*)\qquad \bigl(E_1\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}_S}E_2\bigr)_x \mathrel{\widetilde\longrightarrow} M_1\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}_{A_1}}A \mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}_{A_2}}M_2.\]
The tor-independence hypotheses imply on the other hand:
\[A\mathrel{\widetilde\longrightarrow} A_1\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}_{B_1}}B \mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}_{B_2}}A_2.\]

Consequently, the second member of \((*)\) is canonically isomorphic, in the derived category \(D(B)\) of \(B\)-modules, to \(M_1\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}_{B_1}}B\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}_{B_2}}M_2\). The relations \(\operatorname{tor.amp}_{f_i}(E_i)\subset[a_i,b_i]\) (\(i=1,2\)) imply \(\operatorname{tor.amp}_{B_i}(M_i)\subset[a_i,b_i]\), hence (I 5.5)
\[\operatorname{tor.amp}_B\Bigl( M_1\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}_{B_1}}B \mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}_{B_2}}M_2\Bigr)\subset[a,b],\]

whence assertion b). On the other hand, if we have \(\operatorname{tor.amp}_{f_1}(E_1)\subset[a_1,b_1]\), \(M_1\) is isomorphic to a complex of flat \(B_1\)-modules which are zero outside \([a_1,b_1]\), and consequently if \(E_2\) (hence \(M_2\)) is acyclic outside \([a_2,b_2]\), then \(M_1\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}_{B_1}}B\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}_{B_2}}M_2\) is acyclic outside \([a,b]\), whence assertion a), which completes the proof.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 3.5.1.

Under the hypotheses of (3.5), if \(f_1\) and \(f_2\) are of finite tor-dimension (3.1), the same holds for \(f\).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 3.5.2.

Let

\[\xymatrix{ X\ar[d]_f&X'\ar[l]_g\ar[d]^{f'}\\ Y&Y'\ar[l]^h }\]

be a cartesian square of schemes, where \(X\) and \(Y'\) are assumed tor-independent over \(Y\), and let \(E\in\operatorname{ob}D(X)\). Then we have

\[\operatorname{tor.amp}_f(E)\subset[a,b] \Longrightarrow\operatorname{tor.amp}_{f'}(Lg^*(E))\subset[a,b],\]

and consequently the functor \(Lg^*:D^-(X)\longrightarrow D^-(X')\) induces a functor

\[Lg^*:D(f)_{\mathrm{torf}}\longrightarrow D(f')_{\mathrm{torf}}.\]

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 3.6.

Let

\[\xymatrix{ X\ar[r]^i\ar[d]_f&X'\ar[dl]^{f'}\\ Y& }\]

be a commutative triangle of schemes, where \(i\) is a closed immersion and \(f'\) a smooth morphism, and let \(E\in\operatorname{ob}D(X)\). For \(E\) to be locally of finite flat amplitude rel. to \(f\), it is necessary and sufficient that \(i_*(E)\) be locally of finite flat amplitude (in the absolute sense). More precisely, if \(f'\) is of relative dimension \(\leq d\), we have

(i) \(\operatorname{tor.amp}(i_*(E))\subset[a,b]\Longrightarrow\operatorname{tor.amp}_f(E)\subset[a,b]\),

(ii) \(\operatorname{tor.amp}_f(E)\subset[a,b]\Longrightarrow\operatorname{tor.amp}(i_*(E))\subset[a-d,b]\).
Proof. If \([m,n]\) is an interval of \(\mathbb Z\), we obviously have
\[\operatorname{tor.amp}_f(E)\subset[m,n] \Longleftrightarrow\operatorname{tor.amp}_{f'}(i_*(E))\subset[m,n],\]
so that we may assume that \(X=X'\), \(i=\operatorname{Id}_X\), \(f=f'\). Relation (i) follows trivially from (3.4.1 a)). Let us prove (ii). We have a cartesian square
\[\xymatrix{ X\ar[d]_f&X\times_YX\ar[l]_{\operatorname{pr}_2}\ar[d]^{\operatorname{pr}_1}\\ Y&X\ar[l]^f }\]

and the diagonal morphism \(s:X\longrightarrow X\times_YX\) is a section of \(\operatorname{pr}_1\) and \(\operatorname{pr}_2\). Suppose \(f\) smooth of relative dimension \(d\). Then (VII 1.10) \(s\) is a regular immersion of codimension \(\leq d\), hence is of tor-dimension \(\leq d\) (as one sees by means of the Koszul complex). By (3.5.2), we have
\[\operatorname{tor.amp}_f(E)\subset[a,b] \Longrightarrow\operatorname{tor.amp}_{\operatorname{pr}_1}(\operatorname{pr}_2^*(E))\subset[a,b],\]
and by (3.4.1 b)) we have
\[\operatorname{tor.amp}_{\operatorname{pr}_1}(\operatorname{pr}_2^*(E))\subset[a,b] \Longrightarrow\operatorname{tor.amp}(Ls^*\operatorname{pr}_2^*(E))\subset[a-d,b],\]
whence implication (ii) since \(Ls^*\operatorname{pr}_2^*\mathrel{\widetilde\longrightarrow}\operatorname{Id}\).

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 3.6.1.

The statement (3.6) would be false if one assumed only \(f'\) flat, see (3.4.2).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 3.7. ("Projection formula")(1).

Let \(S\) be a quasi-compact scheme, \(f:X\longrightarrow Y\) a quasi-compact and quasi-separated \(S\)-morphism of quasi-compact schemes over \(S\), \(E\in\operatorname{ob}D^b(X)_{\mathrm{qcoh}}\), \(M\in\operatorname{ob}D^b(S)_{\mathrm{qcoh}}\)(2). If \(M\) has finite flat amplitude, or if \(E\) has finite flat amplitude rel. to \(S\), there exists a canonical functorial isomorphism of \(D(Y)_{\mathrm{qcoh}}\)

\[Rf_*(E)\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}_S}M \mathrel{\widetilde\longrightarrow} Rf_*\bigl(E\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}_S}M\bigr) \qquad\text{(cf. (1.6)).}\]

Proof. Note first that there exists (EGA III 1.4.12 and IV 1.7.21) an integer \(N\) such that we have \(R^if_*(F)=0\) for every \(i>N\) and every quasi-coherent \(\mathcal O_X\)-Module \(F\). Consequently the functor \(Rf_*\) sends \(D^b(X)_{\mathrm{qcoh}}\) into \(D^b(Y)\) (and even \(D^b(Y)_{\mathrm{qcoh}}\) (EGA III 1.4.10 and IV 1.7.21)). Suppose \(E\) (resp. \(M\)) of finite flat amplitude rel. to \(S\) (resp. finite). The adjunction arrow \(Lf^*Rf_*(E)\longrightarrow E\) defines, by tensoring with \(M\), a canonical arrow of \(D(X)\)
\[Lf^*\bigl(Rf_*(E)\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}_S}M\bigr) \longrightarrow E\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}_S}M,\]
where \(E\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}_S}M\in\operatorname{ob}D^b(X)\). By adjunction, one deduces from it a canonical arrow of \(D(Y)\)
\[(*)\qquad Rf_*(E)\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}_S}M \longrightarrow Rf_*\bigl(E\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}_S}M\bigr).\]

The target and the source moreover have quasi-coherent cohomology, since \(Rf_*\) sends \(D^b(X)_{\mathrm{qcoh}}\) into \(D^b(Y)_{\mathrm{qcoh}}\) (EGA III 1.4.10 and IV 1.7.21) and \(\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}_S}\) sends \(D^-(X)_{\mathrm{qcoh}}\times D^-(S)_{\mathrm{qcoh}}\) into \(D^-(X)_{\mathrm{qcoh}}\) (the same with \(Y\) instead of \(X\)). Let us prove that \((*)\) is an isomorphism. The question being local on \(Y\), we may assume \(S\) and \(Y\) affine. By dévissage, we reduce at once, in the non-resp'd case, to the case where \(M\) is an ordinary quasi-coherent \(\mathcal O_S\)-Module. Noting that the functors \(Rf_*(E)\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}_S}\,\cdot\,\) and \(Rf_*(E\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}_S}\,\cdot\,)\) are "way-out left" on the category \(D^b(S)_{\mathrm{qcoh}}\) ([H] I 7), we then reduce, by taking a left resolution of \(M\) by free Modules and then truncating, to the case where \(M\) is free. Since the \(R^if_*\) commute with inductive limits (\(f\) being quasi-compact and quasi-separated), we may even assume \(M\) free of finite type, and then \(M=\mathcal O_S\). The arrow \((*)\) is then tautologically an isomorphism. In the resp'd case, we reduce, by dévissage, to \(M=P\), where \(P\) is a flat \(A\)-module (\(S=\operatorname{Spec}(A)\)). If we set \(A'=D_A(P)\) (EGA 0IV 18.2.3), \(S'=\operatorname{Spec}(A')\), everything comes down to proving that \(Rf_*(E)\) commutes with the flat base change \(S'\longrightarrow S\), i.e. that the canonical arrow
\[q^*Rf_*(E)\longrightarrow Rf'_*p^*(E)\]

is an isomorphism (we have set \(f_{(S')}=f'\) and denoted by \(p\) (resp. \(q\)) the canonical projection \(X_{(S')}\longrightarrow X\) (resp. \(Y_{(S')}\longrightarrow Y\)). But this follows from (EGA III 1.4.15 and IV 1.7.21). This completes the proof of (3.7).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 3.7.1.

Let \(S\) be a scheme, \(f:X\longrightarrow Y\) a quasi-compact and quasi-separated \(S\)-morphism of quasi-compact schemes over \(S\), \(E\in\operatorname{ob}D^b(X)_{\mathrm{qcoh}}\). If \(E\) is locally of finite flat amplitude rel. to \(S\), the same holds for \(Rf_*(E)\). More precisely, if \(S\) is quasi-compact, and if \(N\) is an integer such that \(R^if_*(F)=0\) for every \(i>N\) and every quasi-coherent \(\mathcal O_X\)-Module \(F\) (EGA III 1.4.12 and IV 1.7.21), then we have

\[\operatorname{tor.amp}_S(E)\subset[a,b] \Longrightarrow\operatorname{tor.amp}_S(Rf_*(E))\subset[a,b+N].\]

Proof. We may obviously confine ourselves to proving the last assertion. By (3.3 (i) \(\Longleftrightarrow\) (iv)), and taking into account that \(Rf_*\) "commutes with restriction to an open subset of \(S\)", we must prove that the hypothesis \(\operatorname{tor.amp}_S(E)\subset[a,b]\) implies
\[H^i\bigl(Rf_*(E)\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}_S}M\bigr)=0\]

for every \(i\notin[a,b+N]\) and every quasi-coherent \(\mathcal O_S\)-Module \(M\). Now the preceding hypothesis means that \(H^i(E\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}_S}M)=0\) for every \(i\notin[a,b]\) and every quasi-coherent \(\mathcal O_S\)-Module \(M\), and one concludes thanks to (3.7).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 3.7.2.

Let \(f:X\longrightarrow Y\) be a quasi-compact, quasi-separated morphism which is locally of finite tor-dimension (3.1), and let \(E\in\operatorname{ob}D^b(X)_{\mathrm{qcoh}}\). If \(E\) is locally of finite flat amplitude (in the absolute sense), the same holds for \(Rf_*(E)\).
Proof. Combine (3.4.1 a)) and (3.7.1).

footnotes.html:
cf. ([H] III 5.6).
(2) The subscript "qcoh" indicates the full subcategory consisting of the complexes with quasi-coherent cohomology.

blocks.html: 4. Relative perfection

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 4.1.

Let \(f:X\longrightarrow Y\) be a morphism of schemes locally of finite type, \(E\in\operatorname{ob}D(X)\), \([a,b]\) an interval of \(\mathbb Z\). We say that \(E\) has perfect amplitude relative to \(f\) (or \(Y\) if there is no ambiguity), contained in \([a,b]\), and we write

\[\operatorname{parf.amp}_f(E)\subset[a,b] \qquad\bigl(\text{ou }\operatorname{parf.amp}_Y(E)\subset[a,b]\bigr)\]

if, relative to \(f\), \(E\) is pseudo-coherent (1.2) and has flat amplitude contained in \([a,b]\) (3.1). We say that \(f\) has perfect amplitude contained in \([-n,0]\), or is of perfect dimension \(\leq n\) (\(n\geq0\)), if \(\mathcal O_X\) has perfect amplitude contained in \([-n,0]\). We say that \(E\) has finite perfect amplitude relative to \(f\) if there exists an interval \([a,b]\) such that we have \(\operatorname{parf.amp}_f(E)\subset[a,b]\). We say that \(E\) is perfect rel. to \(f\) if \(E\) is locally of finite perfect amplitude rel. to \(f\). We say that \(f\) is perfect if \(f\) is locally of finite perfect amplitude.

blocks.title: Example
blocks.html:
Example 4.1.1.

A regular immersion of codimension \(d\) (VII 1.4) is of perfect dimension \(\leq d\) (as one sees thanks to the Koszul complex). A morphism which is locally a complete intersection (i.e. locally the composite of a regular immersion and a smooth morphism) is a perfect morphism.
Notation 4.2.

Let \(f:X\longrightarrow Y\) be a morphism of schemes locally of finite type. We shall denote by \(D(f)_{\mathrm{parf}}\), or \(D(X)_{f\text{-parf}}\), or \(D(X)_{Y\text{-parf}}\) if no confusion is to be feared (resp. \(D(f)_{\underline{\mathrm{parf}}}\), \(D(X)_{f\text{-}\underline{\mathrm{parf}}}\), \(D(X)_{Y\text{-}\underline{\mathrm{parf}}}\)) the full subcategory of \(D(X)\) consisting of the complexes that are perfect (resp. of finite perfect amplitude) rel. to \(f\). By (I 5.3) and (1.4), \(D(f)_{\mathrm{parf}}\) and \(D(f)_{\underline{\mathrm{parf}}}\) are triangulated subcategories of \(D(X)\). If \(X\) is quasi-compact, we have \(D(f)_{\mathrm{parf}}=D(f)_{\underline{\mathrm{parf}}}\).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 4.3. (cf. I 5.8).

Let \(f:X\longrightarrow Y\) be a morphism of schemes locally of finite type, \(E\in\operatorname{ob}D(X)\), \([a,b]\) an interval of \(\mathbb Z\), \(x\) a point of \(X\), \(y=f(x)\). The following conditions are equivalent:

(i) \(\operatorname{parf.amp}_f(E)\subset[a,b]\) in a neighborhood of \(x\);

(ii) \(E\) is, in a neighborhood of \(x\), \((a-1)\)-pseudo-coherent rel. to \(f\) and acyclic outside \([a,b]\), and we have \(\operatorname{tor.amp}_{\mathcal O_{Y,y}}(E_x)\subset[a,b]\).

Proof. The implication (i) \(\Longrightarrow\) (ii) is trivial; let us prove (ii) \(\Longrightarrow\) (i). The question being local in a neighborhood of \(x\), we reduce at once, by the definitions, to the case where \(f\) is smooth. Then \(E\) is \((a-1)\)-pseudo-coherent in a neighborhood of \(x\), hence, after localizing if necessary, we may assume \(E\) strictly \((a-1)\)-pseudo-coherent (I 2.1) and zero in degree \(>b\), and after truncating (i.e. replacing \(E\) by the complex \(0\longrightarrow E^a/B^a\longrightarrow E^{a+1}\longrightarrow\cdots\longrightarrow E^b\longrightarrow0\)), we may assume that \(E^i\) is free of finite type for \(i>a\), \(E^a\) of finite presentation, and \(E^i=0\) for \(i\notin[a,b]\). By (I 5.1.1), \(E_x^a\) is flat rel. to \(\mathcal O_{Y,y}\), hence (EGA IV 11.3.1) \(E^a\) is \(f\)-flat in a neighborhood of \(x\). Setting for brevity \(E^a=F\), we must show that \(F\) is pseudo-coherent (in a neighborhood of \(x\)). Embedding \(X\) if necessary in a standard affine space over \(Y\), we may assume that \(X=Y[t_1,\ldots,t_r]\), with \(Y\) affine, \(f\) being the canonical projection. \(Y\) is then a projective limit of noetherian affine schemes \(Y_\alpha\). Denote by \(f_\alpha:X_\alpha=Y_\alpha[t_1,\ldots,t_r]\longrightarrow Y_\alpha\) the canonical projection. There exists (EGA IV 8.5.2 and 11.2.6) an index \(\alpha\) and an \(\mathcal O_{X_\alpha}\)-Module \(F_\alpha\) of finite presentation and \(f_\alpha\)-flat such that \(g_\alpha^*(F_\alpha)\mathrel{\widetilde\longrightarrow}F\), where \(g_\alpha:X\longrightarrow X_\alpha\) is the canonical morphism. Since \(X_\alpha\) is noetherian, \(F_\alpha\) is coherent, hence pseudo-coherent rel. to \(f_\alpha\). Since on the other hand \(F_\alpha\) is \(f_\alpha\)-flat and \(f_\alpha\) is flat, we have
\[g_\alpha^*(F)\mathrel{\widetilde\longrightarrow} F_\alpha\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}_{Y_\alpha}}\mathcal O_Y,\]
and it follows from (1.10 a)) that \(F\) is pseudo-coherent rel. to \(Y\), hence pseudo-coherent, qed.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 4.3.1.

Let \(f:X\longrightarrow Y\) be a morphism locally of finite type, and \(E\) an \(\mathcal O_X\)-Module. For \(\operatorname{parf.amp}_f(E)\subset[0,0]\) to hold, it is necessary and sufficient that \(E\) be, rel. to \(f\), flat and of finite presentation (1.2.1 c)). In particular, for \(f\) to be of perfect dimension zero (4.1), it is necessary and sufficient that \(f\) be flat and locally of finite presentation.
Proof. Apply (4.3 (ii) \(\Longrightarrow\) (i)) with \(a=b=0\).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 4.3.2.

Let \(f:X\longrightarrow Y\) be a morphism locally of finite type, \(E\in\operatorname{ob}D(X)\) a complex of \(\mathcal O_X\)-Modules that are flat and of finite presentation rel. to \(f\) (1.2.1 c)), and \([a,b]\) an interval of \(\mathbb Z\). If \(E^i=0\) for \(i\notin[a,b]\), we have \(\operatorname{parf.amp}_f(E)\subset[a,b]\). If \(E\) has degrees bounded above, \(E\) is pseudo-coherent rel. to \(f\).
Proof. A consequence of (4.3.1) and (I 4.15 and 2.11).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 4.4.

Consider a commutative triangle of schemes

\[\xymatrix{ X\ar[r]^i\ar[dr]_f&Y\ar[d]^g\\ &S, }\]

where \(i\) is a closed immersion and \(g\) a smooth morphism, and let \(E\in\operatorname{ob}D(X)\). For \(E\) to be perfect rel. to \(f\), it is necessary and sufficient that \(i_*(E)\) be perfect \(au sens absolu (I 4.8)\). More precisely, suppose \(g\) of relative dimension \(\leq d\), and let \([a,b]\) be an interval of \(\mathbb Z\). We then have:

(i) \(\operatorname{parf.amp}(i_*(E))\subset[a,b]\Longrightarrow\operatorname{parf.amp}_f(E)\subset[a,b]\);

(ii) \(\operatorname{parf.amp}_f(E)\subset[a,b]\Longrightarrow\operatorname{parf.amp}(i_*(E))\subset[a-d,b]\).
Proof. An immediate consequence of (3.6).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 4.5.

Let

\[\xymatrix{ X\ar[r]^f\ar[dr]_h&Y\ar[d]^g\\ &S }\]

be a commutative triangle of morphisms of schemes locally of finite type, and let \(E\in\operatorname{ob}D^-(X)\), \(F\in\operatorname{ob}D^-(Y)\), \([a,b]\), \([c,d]\) be intervals of \(\mathbb Z\). We then have:

\[\operatorname{parf.amp}_f(E)\subset[a,b]\ \text{ et }\ \operatorname{parf.amp}_g(F)\subset[c,d] \Longrightarrow \operatorname{parf.amp}_h\bigl(E\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}_Y}F\bigr) \subset[a+c,b+d].\]

Consequently, if \(E\) is perfect rel. to \(f\) and \(F\) is perfect rel. to \(g\), then \(E\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}_Y}F\) is perfect rel. to \(h\).
Proof. Combine (1.11) and (3.4).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 4.5.1.

Under the hypotheses of (4.5):

a) if \(g\) is of perfect dimension \(\leq n\), we have

\[\operatorname{parf.amp}_f(E)\subset[a,b] \Longrightarrow\operatorname{parf.amp}_h(E)\subset[a-n,b]\ ;\]

consequently, if \(g\) is perfect, \(E\) perfect rel. to \(f\) implies \(E\) perfect rel. to \(h\);

b) if \(f\) is of perfect dimension \(\leq m\), we have

\[\operatorname{parf.amp}_g(F)\subset[c,d] \Longrightarrow\operatorname{parf.amp}_h(Lf^*(F))\subset[c-m,d]\ ;\]
consequently, if \(f\) is perfect, \(F\) perfect rel. to \(g\) implies \(Lf^*(F)\) perfect rel. to \(h\).

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 4.5.2.

With the preceding notations, if \(g\) is perfect, \(E\) perfect rel. to \(h\) does not necessarily imply \(E\) perfect rel. to \(f\), as is shown by the perennial example of a singular point of a scheme over a field. We have the following criterion:

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 4.6. (criterion of perfection by fibers)(1).

Let

\[\xymatrix{ X\ar[r]^f\ar[dr]_h&Y\ar[d]^g\\ &S }\]

be a commutative triangle of morphisms of schemes. We suppose \(f,h\) locally of finite type, and \(g\) locally of finite presentation and flat. Let \(x\in X\), \(y=f(x)\), \(s=h(x)=g(y)\). Finally, let \(E\in\operatorname{ob}D^-(X)\), and let \([a,b]\) be an interval of \(\mathbb Z\). We denote by \(f_s:X_s\longrightarrow Y_s\) the fiber of \(f\) at \(s\), i.e. the morphism deduced from \(f\) by the base change \(\operatorname{Spec}(k(s))\longrightarrow S\). Then the following conditions are equivalent:

(i) \(\operatorname{parf.amp}_h(E)\subset[a,b]\) in a neighborhood of \(x\), and

\[\operatorname{tor.amp}_{\mathcal O_{Y_s,y}} \Bigl(E_x\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}_{\mathcal O_{Y,y}}} \mathcal O_{Y_s,y}\Bigr)\subset[a,b]\ ;\]

(ii) \(\operatorname{parf.amp}_f(E)\subset[a,b]\) in a neighborhood of \(x\).

Proof. (ii) \(\Longrightarrow\) (i). We already know (4.3.1) that \(g\) is of perfect dimension zero. Hence (4.5.1 a)), condition (ii) implies \(\operatorname{parf.amp}_h(E)\subset[a,b]\) in a neighborhood of \(x\). It also entails, by 4.3), \(\operatorname{tor.amp}_{\mathcal O_{Y,y}}(E_x)\subset[a,b]\), hence (I 5.1 (ii)),
\[\operatorname{tor.amp}_{\mathcal O_{Y_s,y}} \Bigl(E_x\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}_{\mathcal O_{Y,y}}} \mathcal O_{Y_s,y}\Bigr)\subset[a,b],\]
which completes the proof of (ii) \(\Longrightarrow\) (i).

(i) \(\Longrightarrow\) (ii). The question being local in a neighborhood of \(x\), we may assume that \(f\) factors as \(X\xrightarrow{i}X'\xrightarrow{f'}Y\), where \(i\) is a closed immersion and \(f'\) a smooth morphism. It is immediate that conditions (i) and (ii) are not altered when we replace \(E\) by \(i_*(E)\), \(f\) by \(f'\) (and \(h\) by \(gf'\)), so that we may assume \(f\) smooth. Since \(g\) is perfect (4.3.1), \(h\) is perfect, hence (1.12) \(E\) is pseudo-coherent. After localizing if necessary, we may, by (II 2.2.10 b)) and (I 2.10 a)), assume that \(E\) is strictly pseudo-coherent (I 2.1) and zero in degree \(>b\), and that the truncation arrow
\[t):\qquad E\longrightarrow (0\longrightarrow E^a/B^a\longrightarrow E^{a+1}\longrightarrow\cdots \longrightarrow E^b\longrightarrow0)\]

is a quasi-isomorphism (hypothesis (i) indeed implies that \(E\) is, in a neighborhood of \(s\), acyclic outside \([a,b]\)). We must prove that \(E^a/B^a\) is \(f\)-flat in a neighborhood of \(x\). Let us denote by \(i_s:X_s\longrightarrow X\) the canonical arrow. We have:
\[E_x\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}_{\mathcal O_{Y,y}}} \mathcal O_{Y_s,y}\mathrel{\widetilde\longrightarrow}i_s^*(E)_x.\]

Since \(h\) is flat, hypothesis (i) implies, by (3.5.2), \(\operatorname{tor.amp}_{h_s}(i_s^*(E))\subset[a,b]\) in a neighborhood of \(x\). Hence, after localizing if necessary, we may assume that the truncation arrow
\[i_s^*(t):\qquad i_s^*(E)\longrightarrow (0\longrightarrow i_s^*(E^a)/B^a(i_s^*(E))\longrightarrow\cdots \longrightarrow i_s^*(E^b)\longrightarrow0)\]

is a quasi-isomorphism. The hypothesis \(\operatorname{tor.amp}_{\mathcal O_{Y_s,y}}(i_s^*(E)_x)\subset[a,b]\) then entails, by (I 5.1.1), that \(i_s^*(E^a/B^a)_x\) is a flat \(\mathcal O_{Y_s,y}\)-module.

On the other hand, the arrow \((*)\) being a quasi-isomorphism, the hypothesis \(\operatorname{tor.amp}_h(E)\subset[a,b]\) implies, again by (I 5.1.1), that \(E^a/B^a\) is \(h\)-flat. Thanks to the criterion of flatness by fibers (EGA IV 11.3.10), we conclude that \(E^a/B^a\) is \(f\)-flat in a neighborhood of \(x\), qed.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 4.6.1.

Let \(f:X\longrightarrow S\) be a morphism locally of finite presentation and flat, \(x\in X\), \(s=f(x)\), \(E\in\operatorname{ob}D(X)\), and \([a,b]\) an interval of \(\mathbb Z\). Let us denote by \(i_s:X_s=X\times_S\operatorname{Spec}(k(s))\longrightarrow X\) the canonical projection, and set \(E_{X_s}=Li_s^*(E)\). We then have

\[\operatorname{parf.amp}_f(E)\subset[a,b]\text{ au voisinage de }x \ \text{ et }\ \operatorname{tor.amp}((E_{X_s})_x)\subset[a,b]\] \[\Longleftrightarrow\quad \operatorname{parf.amp}(E)\subset[a,b]\text{ au voisinage de }x.\]

Consequently, \(E\) is perfect if and only if \(E\) is perfect rel. to \(f\) and \(E_{X_s}\) is perfect for every \(s\in S\).
Proof. Apply (4.6) with \(X=Y\), \(f=\operatorname{Id}_X\).

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 4.6.2.

From (4.6.1) and (I 5.10) it follows that, if \(f\) is locally of finite presentation, flat and with fibers which are regular schemes, then \(E\) perfect rel. to \(f\) is equivalent to \(E\) perfect (this equivalence had already been obtained (4.4) for \(f\) smooth).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 4.7.

Let \(S\) be a scheme, and, for \(i=1,2\), let \(f_i:X_i\longrightarrow Y_i\) be a \(S\)-morphism locally of finite type, \(E_i\in\operatorname{ob}D(X_i)\), and \([a_i,b_i]\) an interval of \(\mathbb Z\). Suppose \(X_1\) and \(X_2\), as well as \(Y_1\) and \(Y_2\), tor-independent over \(S\) (1.5). Set \(a=a_1+a_2\), \(b=b_1+b_2\), \(f=f_1\times_Sf_2\). Then we have:

a) If \(\operatorname{parf.amp}_{f_1}(E_1)\subset[a_1,b_1]\), and if \(E_2\) is pseudo-coherent rel. to \(f_2\) and acyclic in degrees outside \([a_2,b_2]\), then \(E_1\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}_S}E_2\) (cf. (1.6)) is pseudo-coherent rel. to \(f\) and acyclic in degrees outside \([a,b]\).

b) \(\operatorname{parf.amp}_{f_i}(E_i)\subset[a_i,b_i]\) for \(i=1,2\) implies

\[\operatorname{parf.amp}_f\bigl(E_1\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}_S}E_2\bigr)\subset[a,b].\]
Proof. Combine (1.9) and (3.5).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 4.7.1.

Under the hypotheses of (4.6), if, for \(i=1,2\), \(f_i\) is of perfect dimension \(\leq n_i\), then \(f\) is of perfect dimension \(\leq n_1+n_2\). Consequently, if \(f_1\) and \(f_2\) are perfect, so is \(f\).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 4.7.2.

Let

\[\xymatrix{ X\ar[d]_f&X'\ar[l]_g\ar[d]^{f'}\\ Y&Y'\ar[l]^h }\]

be a cartesian square of schemes, where \(f\) is locally of finite type and \(X\) and \(Y'\) are assumed tor-independent over \(Y\), and let \(E\in\operatorname{ob}D(X)\), and \([a,b]\) an interval of \(\mathbb Z\). We have:

\[\operatorname{parf.amp}_f(E)\subset[a,b] \Longrightarrow\operatorname{parf.amp}_{f'}(Lg^*(E))\subset[a,b],\]

and consequently the functor \(Lg^*:D^-(X)\longrightarrow D^-(X')\) sends \(D(f)_{\mathrm{parf}}\) (resp. \(D(f)_{\underline{\mathrm{parf}}}\)) into \(D(f')_{\mathrm{parf}}\) (resp. \(D(f')_{\underline{\mathrm{parf}}}\)) (4.2).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 4.8.

Let \(S\) be a scheme, \(f:X\longrightarrow Y\) a proper \(S\)-morphism of \(S\)-schemes locally of finite type, \(E\in\operatorname{ob}D^+(X)\). Then, subject to the validity of (2.1) for \(f\) (hence in any case if \(S\) is locally noetherian or if \(f\) is projective):

a) If \(E\) is perfect rel. to \(S\), so is \(Rf_*(E)\).

b) Suppose \(S\) quasi-compact, and \(X\) and \(Y\) of finite type over \(S\); let \([a,b]\) be an interval of \(\mathbb Z\), and \(N\) an integer such that \(R^if_*(M)=0\) for every \(i>N\) and every quasi-coherent \(\mathcal O_X\)-Module \(M\) (such an integer exists by (EGA III 1.4.12 and IV 1.7.21)); we then have

\[ \operatorname{parf.amp}_S(E)\subset[a,b]\Longrightarrow \operatorname{parf.amp}_S(Rf_*(E))\subset[a,b+N]. \]

Consequently, the functor \(Rf_*\) sends \(D(X)_{S\text{-}\mathrm{parf}}\) into \(D(Y)_{S\text{-}\mathrm{parf}}\)(1).
Proof. Assertion a) follows from b), and b) is obtained by combining (2.1) and (3.7.1).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 4.8.1.

Let \(f:X\longrightarrow Y\) be a proper and perfect morphism, and \(E\in\operatorname{ob}D^+(X)\). Then, subject to the validity of (2.1) for \(f\), if \(E\) is perfect, so is \(Rf_*(E)\); moreover, if \(Y\) is quasi-compact, the functor \(Rf_*\) sends \(D(X)_{\mathrm{parf}}\) into \(D(Y)_{\mathrm{parf}}\)(2).
Proof. Indeed, if \(E\) is perfect, \(E\) is perfect rel. to \(f\) by (4.5.1 a)).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 4.9(3).

Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, \(X\) and \(Y\) \(S\)-schemes of finite type, \(f:X\longrightarrow Y\) a compactifiable \(S\)-morphism (i.e. the composite of an open immersion and a proper morphism), \(E\in\operatorname{ob}D(X)\), \(F\in\operatorname{ob}D^+(Y)\). If \(E\) is perfect rel. to \(f\), and \(F\) perfect rel. to \(S\), then \(\mathbf R\!\operatorname{Hom}(E,f^!(F))\) is perfect rel. to \(S\)(4).

Proof. The question being local on \(X\), we may assume that \(f\) factors as \(X\xrightarrow{i}X'\xrightarrow{f'}Y\), where \(i\) is a closed immersion and \(f'\) a smooth morphism. If \(E\) is perfect rel. to \(f\), then \(E\) is perfect rel. to \(i\) (4.4), and if \(F\) is perfect rel. to \(S\), then \(f'^!(F)\) is perfect rel. to \(S\) by (4.5.1 b)) since \(f'^!(F)\) is locally isomorphic to \(f'^*(F)\) up to translation. Since we have \(f^!\simeq i^!f'^!\), we are thus reduced to proving the assertion in the case where \(f\) is a closed immersion. Suppose then \(E\) perfect rel. to \(f\), i.e. \(f_*(E)\) perfect, and \(F\) perfect rel. to \(S\). We must show that \(f_*\mathbf R\!\operatorname{Hom}(E,f^!F)\) is perfect rel. to \(S\). But by the duality theorem ([H] III 6.7), we have
\[ f_*\mathbf R\!\operatorname{Hom}(E,f^!F) \mathrel{\widetilde\longrightarrow} \mathbf R\!\operatorname{Hom}(f_*(E),F). \]
Since \(f_*(E)\) is perfect, we have, by (I 7.7),
\[ \mathbf R\!\operatorname{Hom}(f_*(E),F) \mathrel{\widetilde\longrightarrow} (f_*(E))^\vee\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}}F, \]
and we are done thanks to (4.5) (where \(X=Y\) and \(f=\operatorname{Id}_X\)).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 4.9.1.

Under the hypotheses of (4.8), if \(f\) is perfect, \(f^!\) sends \(D(Y)_{S\text{-}\mathrm{parf}}\) into \(D(X)_{S\text{-}\mathrm{parf}}\).
Proof. Take \(E=\mathcal O_X\).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 4.9.2.

Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme and \(f:X\longrightarrow S\) a compactifiable morphism. Set \(f^!(\mathcal O_S)=K_X\), \(\mathbf R\!\operatorname{Hom}(\,\cdot\,,K_X)=\mathbb D_X\). For \(E\in\operatorname{ob}D(f)_{\mathrm{parf}}\), we have \(\mathbb D_X(E)\in\operatorname{ob}D(f)_{\mathrm{parf}}\), and the canonical arrow (I 7.2)

\[ E\longrightarrow\mathbb D_X\mathbb D_X(E) \]

is an isomorphism.

Proof. For the first assertion, apply (4.8) with \(Y=S\), \(F=\mathcal O_S\). For the second, one reduces easily to the case where \(f\) is a closed immersion. It then suffices to prove that the arrow deduced from the canonical arrow by applying \(f_*\) is an isomorphism. By global duality ([H] III 6.7), we have
\[ f_*\mathbb D_X\mathrel{\widetilde\longrightarrow}\mathbb D_Sf_*. \]
We are thus reduced to showing that the canonical arrow
\[ f_*(E)\longrightarrow\mathbb D_S\mathbb D_Sf_*(E) \]
is an isomorphism for \(E\) perfect rel. to \(f\), i.e. \(f_*(E)\) perfect, which follows from ordinary biduality for perfect complexes (I 7.2).

5. Applications: exchange and semi-continuity theorems(1).

Lemma 5.1.

Let \((X,\mathcal O_X)\) be a (commutative) ringed topos, let us denote by \(\operatorname{Mod}(X)\) the category of \(\mathcal O_X\)-Modules, \(D(X)=D(\operatorname{Mod}(X))\). Let \(E\in\operatorname{ob}D^-(X)\), and \(p\in\mathbb Z\). Consider the following conditions:

a) The functor \(M\longmapsto H^p(E\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}}M)\) from \(\operatorname{Mod}(X)\) to \(\operatorname{Mod}(X)\) is right exact.

a') The canonical arrow

\[ H^p(E)\otimes M\longrightarrow H^p(E\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}}M) \]

is an isomorphism for every \(M\in\operatorname{ob}\operatorname{Mod}(X)\).

b) The functor \(M\longmapsto H^{p+1}(E\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}}M)\) from \(\operatorname{Mod}(X)\) to \(\operatorname{Mod}(X)\) is left exact.

b') The canonical arrow defined below

\[ (*)\qquad H^{p+1}(E\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}}M) \longrightarrow \operatorname{Hom}(H^{-p-1}(E^\vee),M) \]

is an isomorphism for every \(M\in\operatorname{ob}\operatorname{Mod}(X)\).

Then we have:

\[ \mathrm{a)}\Longleftrightarrow\mathrm{a')} \Longleftrightarrow\mathrm{b)}\Longleftarrow\mathrm{b')}. \]

Moreover, if \(E\) is pseudo-coherent, the conditions a), a'), b), b') are equivalent.

Definition of \((*)\). The canonical arrow (I 7.5) \(E^\vee\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}}E\longrightarrow\mathcal O_X\), where \(E^\vee=\mathbf R\!\operatorname{Hom}(E,\mathcal O_X)\), gives, by tensoring with \(M\in\operatorname{ob}\operatorname{Mod}(X)\), an arrow

\[ E^\vee\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}}E \mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}}M\longrightarrow M, \]

whence, by the adjunction formula dear to Cartan (I 7.4), a canonical arrow

\[ (**)\qquad E\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}}M \longrightarrow\mathbf R\!\operatorname{Hom}(E^\vee,M). \]

Composing the arrow induced by \((**)\) on \(H^{p+1}\) with the canonical arrow

\[ (***)\qquad \operatorname{Ext}^{p+1}(E^\vee,M) \longrightarrow\operatorname{Hom}(H^{-p-1}(E^\vee),M), \]

we obtain the arrow \((*)\).

Proof. The implications

\[ \mathrm{a')}\Longrightarrow\mathrm{a)} \Longleftrightarrow\mathrm{b)} \Longleftarrow\mathrm{b')} \]

are trivial. Let us prove a) \(\Longrightarrow\) a'). Let \(M\) be a \(\mathcal O_X\)-Module. There exists an exact sequence \(M^1\longrightarrow M^0\longrightarrow M\longrightarrow0\), where \(M^0\) and \(M^1\) are direct sums of sheaves of the type \(\mathcal O_{U,X}\), where \(U\) is an object of \(X\). Using the right exactness and the commutation with direct sums of the functors \(H^p(E)\otimes\,\cdot\,\) and \(H^p(E\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}}\,\cdot\,)\), we are reduced to proving that the arrow of a') is an isomorphism for \(M=\mathcal O_{U,X}\), \(U\in\operatorname{ob}X\). Now \(H^p(E)\otimes\mathcal O_{U,X}\) (resp. \(H^p(E\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}}\mathcal O_{U,X})\)) is canonically identified (SGA 4 IV 2.13) with \(i_!(H^p(E)|U)\) (resp. \(H^p(i_!(E|U))\)), where \(i_!\) is the extension by \(0\) outside \(U\), and we are done because \(i_!\) is exact.

Suppose \(E\) pseudo-coherent, and let us prove b) \(\Longrightarrow\) b'). It obviously suffices to show that \((*)\) is an isomorphism for \(M\) injective. Since the arrow \((***)\) is an isomorphism for \(M\) injective, the assertion will thus follow from the following one:

(5.1.1): the arrow \((**)\) is an isomorphism if \(E\) is pseudo-coherent and \(M\) injective.

Proof of (5.1.1). Let us denote by \(T\) (resp. \(T'\)) the functor from \(D^-(X)\) to \(D(X)\) defined by \(F\longmapsto F\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}}M\) (resp. \(\mathbf R\!\operatorname{Hom}(F^\vee,M)\)). Let \(n\in\mathbb Z\); if \(H^i(F)=0\) for \(i>n\), then \(H^iT(F)=0\) for \(i>n\), \(H^i(F^\vee)=0\) for \(i<-n\), and \(H^iT'(F)=0\) for \(i>n\) if \(M\) is injective. Since on the other hand the arrow \((**)\) commutes with restriction to an open object, and since an injective restricted to an open object is again injective, we may, after localizing and truncating, assume \(E\) strictly perfect (2.1). By dévissage, we then reduce to \(E=\mathcal O_X\), and we are done.

Remarks 5.2.

(i) Condition a) (resp. a'), b), b')) is of a local nature: if \((X_\alpha)\) is an open covering of the final object of \(X\), and if, for each \(\alpha\), condition a) (resp. ...) is satisfied for \(X_\alpha\) and \(E|X_\alpha\), then a) (resp. ...) is satisfied.

(ii) If \(x\) is a point of \(X\), let us denote by \(\mathrm a_x\) (resp. \(\mathrm a'_x\), etc.) the condition a) (resp. a'), etc.) relative to \(E_x\) and \(\operatorname{Mod}(\mathcal O_{X,x})\). Then, if \(X\) has enough points, condition a) (resp. a'), b)) is equivalent to the condition "for every point \(x\) of \(X\), \(\mathrm a_x\) (resp. \(\mathrm a'_x\), \(\mathrm b_x\))", and is equivalent to the condition "for every point \(x\) of \(X\), \(\mathrm b'_x\)" if \(E\) is pseudo-coherent; moreover condition a') is equivalent to the analogous condition where one replaces "for every \(M\in\operatorname{ob}\operatorname{Mod}(X)\)" by "for every \(\mathcal O_X\)-Module which is the cokernel of a homomorphism \(\mathcal O_{U,X}^p\longrightarrow\mathcal O_{U,X}^q\), where \(U\) is an object of \(X\)".

(iii) The criterion (EGA III 7.4.2) extends word for word to our situation: if \(E\) has flat components, condition a) simply means that \(\operatorname{Coker}(E^p\longrightarrow E^{p+1})\) is flat, hence locally a direct factor of a free Module of finite type if \(E^p\) and \(E^{p+1}\) are moreover assumed to be of finite presentation (I 5.8.3). We deduce from this in particular that, if \(E\) is perfect, then \(E\) satisfies condition a) relative to the integer \(p\) if and only if \(E^\vee\) satisfies condition a) relative to the integer \(-p-1\).

(iv) Let \(X\) be a quasi-separated scheme, \(\mathcal O_X\) the structure sheaf, \(E\in\operatorname{ob}D^-(X)\) a complex with locally bounded and quasi-coherent cohomology. Let us denote by \(\mathrm a_{\mathrm{qcoh}}\) (resp. \(\mathrm a'_{\mathrm{qcoh}}\), etc.) the condition analogous to a) (resp. a'), etc.) where one replaces \(\operatorname{Mod}(X)\) by the category \(\operatorname{Qcoh}(X)\) of quasi-coherent \(\mathcal O_X\)-Modules. Then we have

\[ \mathrm a)\Longleftrightarrow\mathrm a_{\mathrm{qcoh}}) \Longleftrightarrow\mathrm a'_{\mathrm{qcoh}}) \Longleftrightarrow\mathrm b_{\mathrm{qcoh}}) \Longleftarrow\mathrm b'_{\mathrm{qcoh}}), \]

and the preceding conditions are equivalent if \(E\) is pseudo-coherent.

Indeed, since \(X\) is quasi-separated, every quasi-coherent sheaf on an affine open subset of \(X\) is induced by a quasi-coherent sheaf on \(X\) (EGA I 9.4.2 and IV 1.2.7). It follows that condition \(\mathrm a_{\mathrm{qcoh}}\) (resp. \(\mathrm a'_{\mathrm{qcoh}}\), \(\mathrm b_{\mathrm{qcoh}}\), \(\mathrm b'_{\mathrm{qcoh}}\)) is satisfied if and only if it is satisfied above every affine open subset. To prove the implications above, we may therefore assume \(X\) affine. Then (II 3.5) \(E\) is isomorphic in \(D(X)\) to a complex of quasi-coherent Modules, and the implications

\[ \mathrm a_{\mathrm{qcoh}}) \Longleftrightarrow\mathrm a'_{\mathrm{qcoh}}) \Longleftrightarrow\mathrm b_{\mathrm{qcoh}}) \Longleftarrow\mathrm b'_{\mathrm{qcoh}}) \]

follow from (5.1) applied to the "punctual" topos ringed by \(\Gamma(X,\mathcal O_X)\); the same holds, by virtue of (II 2.2.10 a)), for the implication \(\mathrm b_{\mathrm{qcoh}}\Longrightarrow\mathrm b'_{\mathrm{qcoh}}\) for \(E\) pseudo-coherent. Finally, the equivalence \(\mathrm a)\Longleftrightarrow\mathrm a_{\mathrm{qcoh}}\), or, what amounts to the same, \(\mathrm a')\Longleftrightarrow\mathrm a'_{\mathrm{qcoh}}\), follows from the last remark of (ii) (and from the fact that quasi-coherent sheaves "extend", \(X\) being quasi-separated).

Proposition 5.3. (exchange property) (EGA III 7.7.5 II).

Let \(f:X\longrightarrow Y\) be a morphism of quasi-compact and quasi-separated schemes, \(E\in\operatorname{ob}D^b(X)\) a complex with quasi-coherent cohomology and of finite flat amplitude rel. to \(f\), and \(p\in\mathbb Z\). Consider the following conditions:

a) The functor \(M\longmapsto R^pf_*(E\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}_Y}M)\) from \(\operatorname{Qcoh}(Y)\)(1) to \(\operatorname{Qcoh}(Y)\)(2) is right exact.

a') The canonical arrow

\[ R^pf_*(E)\otimes M\longrightarrow R^pf_*(E\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}_Y}M) \]

(composed of the canonical arrow \(R^pf_*(E)\otimes M\longrightarrow H^p(Rf_*(E)\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}}M)\) and of the isomorphism (3.7) \(H^p(Rf_*(E)\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}}M)\mathrel{\widetilde\longrightarrow}R^pf_*(E\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}_Y}M)\)) is an isomorphism for every \(M\in\operatorname{ob}\operatorname{Qcoh}(Y)\).

b) The functor \(M\longmapsto R^{p+1}f_*(E\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}_Y}M)\) from \(\operatorname{Qcoh}(Y)\) to \(\operatorname{Qcoh}(Y)\) is left exact.

b') The canonical arrow

\[ R^{p+1}f_*(E\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}_Y}M) \longrightarrow \operatorname{Hom}(H^{-p-1}((Rf_*(E))^\vee),M) \]
