(composed of the isomorphism (3.7) \(R^{p+1}f_*(E\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}_Y}M)\mathrel{\widetilde\longrightarrow}H^{p+1}(Rf_*(E)\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}}M)\) and of the canonical arrow (5.1 \((*)\)) \(H^{p+1}(Rf_*(E)\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}}M)\longrightarrow\operatorname{Hom}(H^{-p-1}((Rf_*(E))^\vee),M)\)) is an isomorphism for every \(M\in\operatorname{ob}\operatorname{Qcoh}(Y)\).

c) For every base change \(Y'\longrightarrow Y\), the canonical arrow

\[ R^pf_*(E)\otimes_Y\mathcal O_{Y'} \longrightarrow H^p\bigl(Rf_*(E)\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}_Y}\mathcal O_{Y'}\bigr) \]

is an isomorphism.

Then we have:

\[ \mathrm a)\Longleftrightarrow\mathrm a') \Longleftrightarrow\mathrm b) \Longleftrightarrow\mathrm c) \Longleftarrow\mathrm b'). \]

Moreover, if \(f\) is proper, \(E\) pseudo-coherent rel. to \(f\) (hence perfect rel. to \(f\)), and subject to the validity of (2.1) for \(f\) (hence in particular if \(f\) is projective or if \(Y\) is noetherian), the preceding conditions are equivalent.

Proof. The implications \(\mathrm a)\Longleftrightarrow\mathrm a')\Longleftrightarrow\mathrm b)\Longleftarrow\mathrm b')\) follow at once from (3.7), (5.1) and (5.2 (iv)), as does the implication \(\mathrm b')\Longleftarrow\mathrm b)\) (for \(f\) proper and \(E\) pseudo-coherent rel. to \(f\)) by virtue of (2.1). It remains to prove that we have \(\mathrm a)\Longleftrightarrow\mathrm c)\). This will follow from the first assertion of the following lemma:

Lemma 5.3.1.

Let \(X\) be a quasi-separated scheme, and let \(E\in\operatorname{ob}D^-(X)\) be a complex with locally bounded and quasi-coherent cohomology. Then condition (5.1 a)) is equivalent to the condition

c) For every scheme \(X'\) over \(X\), the canonical arrow

\[ H^p(E)\otimes_X\mathcal O_{X'} \longrightarrow H^p(E\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}_X}\mathcal O_{X'}) \]

is an isomorphism.

Moreover, if \(E\) is pseudo-coherent, condition (5.1 a)) implies that, for every morphism \(X'\longrightarrow X\), the canonical arrow

\[ \tag{5.3.1.1} H^{-p-1}(E^\vee)\otimes_X\mathcal O_{X'} \longrightarrow H^{-p-1}\bigl((E\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}_X}\mathcal O_{X'})^\vee\bigr), \]

composed of \(H^{-p-1}(E^\vee)\otimes_X\mathcal O_{X'}\longrightarrow H^{-p-1}(E^\vee\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}_X}\mathcal O_{X'})\) and of the canonical arrow (I 7.1.2) \(H^{-p-1}(E^\vee\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}_X}\mathcal O_{X'})\longrightarrow H^{-p-1}((E\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}_X}\mathcal O_{X'})^\vee)\), is an isomorphism.

Proof. a) \(\Longrightarrow\) c). The question being local on \(X'\), we may assume \(X\) (resp. \(X'\)) affine with ring \(A\) (resp. \(A'\)). By (II 3.5), we may assume on the other hand that we have \(E=F^\sim\), where \(F\) is a complex of \(A\)-modules. By hypothesis (condition a')) the arrow

\[ H^p(F)\otimes_AA'\longrightarrow H^p(F\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}_A}A') \]

is an isomorphism, and we are done since \(H^p(E)\otimes_X\mathcal O_{X'}\) (resp. \(H^p(E\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}_X}\mathcal O_{X'})\)) is canonically identified with the sheaf on \(X'\) defined by the \(A'\)-module \(H^p(F)\otimes_AA'\) (resp. \(H^p(F\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}_A}A')\)).

c) \(\Longrightarrow\) a). Taking (5.2 (iv)) into account, it suffices to prove c) \(\Longrightarrow\) \(\mathrm a'_{\mathrm{qcoh}}\). The question being local (since \(X\) is quasi-separated), we may assume \(X\) affine with ring \(A\), and \(E=F^\sim\), where \(F\) is a complex of \(A\)-modules. Let \(M=N^\sim\) be a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal O_X\)-Module. We must show that the arrow

\[ H^p(F)\otimes_AN\longrightarrow H^p(F\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}_A}N) \]

is an isomorphism. But, if we set \(A'=D_A(N)\) (EGA \(0_{\mathrm{IV}}\) 18.2.3), \(X'=\operatorname{Spec}(A')\), this follows from c) applied to the canonical morphism \(X'\longrightarrow X\).

Let us prove the last assertion. The question being local on \(X'\), we may, by (II 2.2.10 b)), assume \(E\) strictly pseudo-coherent. Moreover, since the assertion involves only a part of bounded length of \(E\), we may, after truncating, assume \(E\) strictly perfect. Then \((E\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}_X}\mathcal O_{X'})^\vee\) is canonically identified (I 7.1.2) with \(E^\vee\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}_X}\mathcal O_{X'}\), and the arrow (5.3.1.1) can be rewritten as the canonical arrow

\[ (*)\qquad H^{-p-1}(E^\vee)\otimes_X\mathcal O_{X'} \longrightarrow H^{-p-1}(E^\vee\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}_X}\mathcal O_{X'}). \]

If \(E\) satisfies condition (5.1 a)) for the integer \(p\), \(E^\vee\) satisfies the same condition for the integer \(-p-1\) by virtue of (5.2 (iii)), and the arrow \((*)\) is an isomorphism, by the first assertion of (5.3.1). This completes the proof of (5.3.1), and consequently of (5.3).

Remark 5.3.2.

Proposition (5.3) applies in particular when \(E\) is a complex in bounded degrees of quasi-coherent \(\mathcal O_X\)-Modules which are flat and of finite presentation rel. to \(f\): indeed, \(E\) is then of finite perfect amplitude rel. to \(f\) by virtue of (4.3.2).

Definition 5.4.

Let \(X\) be a topos, and let us denote by \(\operatorname{Pt}(X)\) the set of its points (SGA 4 IV 6.1). Let \(M\) be an ordered set, \(f:\operatorname{Pt}(X)\longrightarrow M\) a map, and \(x\in\operatorname{Pt}(X)\). We say that \(f\) is upper semi-continuous (resp. lower semi-continuous, resp. locally constant) at \(x\) if there exists a \(x\)-pointed object \(U\) of \(X\) such that for every point \(y\) of \(U\) we have \(f(y)\leq f(x)\) (resp. \(f(y)\geq f(x)\), resp. \(f(y)=f(x)\)). We say that \(f\) is upper semi-continuous (resp. lower semi-continuous, resp. locally constant) if it is so at every point(1).

Examples 5.4.1.

a) Let us denote by \(M_X\) the constant sheaf with value \(M\) on \(X\), and let \(s\in H^0(X,M)\). The map \(x\longmapsto s(x)=x^*(s)\) from \(\operatorname{Pt}(X)\) to \(M\) is locally constant. In particular, let \(E\) be a perfect complex on \(X\) assumed locally ringed: the rank of \(E\) (I 6.11) defines a locally constant function on \(\operatorname{Pt}(X)\) with values in \(\mathbb Z\).

b) Suppose \(X\) locally ringed, and let \(P\) be a \(\mathcal O_X\)-Module of finite type. For \(x\in\operatorname{Pt}(X)\), let us denote by \(\mathcal O_{X,x}\) the stalk local ring of \(\mathcal O_X\) at \(x\), and by \(k(x)\) the residue field. Then, for \(x\in\operatorname{Pt}(X)\), \(P_x\otimes_{\mathcal O_{X,x}}k(x)\) is of finite rank over \(k(x)\), and the function \(x\longmapsto\operatorname{rg}_{k(x)}(P_x\otimes_{\mathcal O_{X,x}}k(x))\) is upper semi-continuous. Indeed, if, for \(x\in\operatorname{Pt}(X)\), \(P_x\otimes_{\mathcal O_{X,x}}k(x)\) is of rank \(n\), there exists an epimorphism \(\mathcal O_{X,x}^n\longrightarrow P_x\) by Nakayama, and, since \(P\) is of finite type, there exists a \(x\)-pointed object \(U\) of \(X\) and an epimorphism \(\mathcal O_U^n\longrightarrow P|U\), whence the assertion.

Lemma 5.5.

Let \((X,\mathcal O_X)\) be a locally ringed topos, \(E\in\operatorname{ob}D^-(X)\), and \(p\in\mathbb Z\). Then, with the notations of (5.4.1 b)), we have:

(i) If \(E\) is perfect, the \(H^i(E_x\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}_{\mathcal O_{X,x}}}k(x))\), for \(x\in\operatorname{Pt}(X)\), are vector spaces of finite rank over \(k(x)\), zero except for a finite number, and the function

\[ x\longmapsto\sum(-1)^i\operatorname{rg}_{k(x)} H^i(E_x\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}_{\mathcal O_{X,x}}}k(x)) \]

is locally constant on \(\operatorname{Pt}(X)\).

(ii) If \(E\) is \((p-1)\)-pseudo-coherent, \(H^p(E_x\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}_{\mathcal O_{X,x}}}k(x))\) is, for \(x\in\operatorname{Pt}(X)\), a vector space of finite rank over \(k(x)\), and the function

\[ x\longmapsto\operatorname{rg}_{k(x)} H^p(E_x\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}_{\mathcal O_{X,x}}}k(x)) \]

is upper semi-continuous on \(\operatorname{Pt}(X)\). If moreover the functor \(M\longmapsto H^p(E\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}}M)\) from \(\operatorname{Mod}(X)\) to \(\operatorname{Mod}(X)\) is exact, \(H^p(E)\) is locally free of finite type and the preceding function is locally constant.

(iii) Suppose \(E\) is \(p\)-pseudo-coherent (resp. \((p-1)\)-pseudo-coherent), and let \(x\in\operatorname{Pt}(X)\). If the functor \(M\longmapsto H^p(E_x\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}_{\mathcal O_{X,x}}}M)\) is right exact (resp. left exact) on the category \(\operatorname{Mod}(x)\) of \(\mathcal O_{X,x}\)-Modules, there exists a \(x\)-pointed open object \(U\) of \(X\) such that the functor \(M\longmapsto H^p(E|U\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}}M)\) is right exact (resp. left exact) on \(\operatorname{Mod}(U)\).

Proof. (i) By (I 4.19.1), \(E_x\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}_{\mathcal O_{X,x}}}k(x)\) is a perfect complex of \(k(x)\)-modules, hence (I 5.9 and 3.5) the \(H^i(E_x\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}_{\mathcal O_{X,x}}}k(x))\) are of finite rank over \(k(x)\) and zero except for a finite number. We have

\[ \tag{5.5.1} \operatorname{rg}(E_x\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}_{\mathcal O_{X,x}}}k(x)) =\sum(-1)^i\operatorname{rg}_{k(x)} H^i(E_x\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}_{\mathcal O_{X,x}}}k(x)), \]

as one sees by an immediate dévissage using the additivity of the rank function. Taking (5.4.1 a)) into account, assertion (i) thus follows from the compatibility of the rank with inverse images (I 6.12).

(ii) After truncating and translating, we may, for the first assertion, assume \(E\) strictly perfect, \(p=0\), and \(E^i=0\) for \(i\notin[-1,+1]\). For \(x\in\operatorname{Pt}(X)\), set \(E_x\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}_{\mathcal O_{X,x}}}k(x)=E_{k(x)}\). By (5.5.1) we have

\[ \operatorname{rg}_{k(x)}H^0(E_{k(x)}) =\operatorname{rg}_{k(x)}H^{-1}(E_{k(x)}) +\operatorname{rg}_{k(x)}H^1(E_{k(x)}) +\operatorname{rg}(E_x), \]

so it suffices to prove that the function \(x\longmapsto\operatorname{rg}_{k(x)}H^{-1}(E_{k(x)})\) (resp. \(\operatorname{rg}_{k(x)}H^1(E_{k(x)})\)) is upper semi-continuous (since the function \(x\longmapsto\operatorname{rg}(E_x)=\operatorname{rg}(E_{k(x)})\) is locally constant). For the function \(x\longmapsto\operatorname{rg}_{k(x)}H^1(E_{k(x)})\), this is an immediate consequence of (5.4.1 b))(*). For the function \(x\longmapsto\operatorname{rg}_{k(x)}H^{-1}(E_{k(x)})\), we may assume \(E^1=0\), i.e. replace \(E\) by \(E'=(0\longrightarrow E^{-1}\longrightarrow E^0\longrightarrow0)\). We already know that the function \(x\longmapsto\operatorname{rg}_{k(x)}H^0(E'_{k(x)})\) is upper semi-continuous, hence so is the function \(x\longmapsto\operatorname{rg}_{k(x)}H^{-1}(E'_{k(x)})=\operatorname{rg}_{k(x)}H^{-1}(E_{k(x)})\), since the function \(x\longmapsto\operatorname{rg}_{k(x)}H^0(E'_{k(x)})-\operatorname{rg}_{k(x)}H^{-1}(E'_{k(x)})=\operatorname{rg}(E'_x)\) is locally constant.

Let us now prove the second assertion. After truncating, we may assume \(E\) strictly perfect and \(E^i=0\) for \(i<p-1\). The \(\mathcal O_X\)-Module \(Z'^i(E)=\operatorname{Coker}(E^{i-1}\longrightarrow E^i)\) is therefore of finite presentation. We have an exact sequence:

\[ (*)\qquad 0\longrightarrow H^p(E)\longrightarrow Z'^p(E)\longrightarrow E^{p+1} \longrightarrow Z'^{p+1}(E)\longrightarrow0. \]

Since the functor \(M\longmapsto H^p(E\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}}M)\) is right and left exact, \(Z'^{p+1}(E)\) and \(Z'^p(E)\) are flat by (5.2 (iii)), hence (I 5.8.3) locally free of finite type. The exactness of \((*)\) then implies that \(H^p(E)\) is locally free of finite type, and consequently that the function \(x\longmapsto\operatorname{rg}_{k(x)}H^p(E_{k(x)})=\operatorname{rg}(H^p(E)_x)\)

is locally constant, which completes the proof of (ii).

(iii) We may assume \(E\) strictly perfect and \(E^i=0\) for \(i<p\) (resp. \(p-1\)). To say that the functor \(M\longmapsto H^p(E_x\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}_{\mathcal O_{X,x}}}M)\) is right exact (resp. left exact) on \(\operatorname{Mod}(x)\) is equivalent, by (5.2 (iii)), to saying that \(Z'^{p+1}(E_x)\) (resp. \(Z'^p(E_x)\)) (with the notations of (ii)) is flat, hence free of finite type (being of finite presentation). Since we have \(Z'^{p+1}(E_x)\mathrel{\widetilde\longrightarrow}Z'^{p+1}(E)_x\) (resp. \(Z'^p(E_x)\mathrel{\widetilde\longrightarrow}Z'^p(E)_x\)), the usual argument (cf. EGA \(0_{\mathrm I}\) 5.2.7) shows that there exists a \(x\)-pointed object \(U\) of \(X\) such that \(Z'^{p+1}(E|U)\) (resp. \(Z'^p(E|U)\)) is free of finite type, and we are done.

Corollary 5.6. (EGA III 7.6.9).

Let \(X\) be a scheme, \(p\in\mathbb Z\), and \(E\in\operatorname{ob}D^-(X)\) a \((p-1)\)-pseudo-coherent complex. Then:

(i) For \(x\in X\), \(H^p(E\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}_X}k(x))\) is of finite rank over \(k(x)\) and the function

\[ x\longmapsto\operatorname{rg}_{k(x)} H^p(E\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}_X}k(x)) \]

is constructible and upper semi-continuous on \(X\).

(ii) Suppose \(X\) quasi-separated and \(E\) with quasi-coherent and locally bounded cohomology. If the functor \(M\longmapsto H^p(E\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}}M)\) is exact on the category of quasi-coherent \(\mathcal O_X\)-Modules, the function \(x\longmapsto\operatorname{rg}_{k(x)}H^p(E\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}_X}k(x))\) is locally constant and \(H^p(E)\) is locally free of finite type. Conversely, if \(X\) is locally noetherian and reduced and if the preceding function is locally constant, the functor \(M\longmapsto H^p(E\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}}M)\) is exact on the category of quasi-coherent \(\mathcal O_X\)-Modules.

Proof. If \(X\) is integral with generic point \(x\), the functor \(M\longmapsto H^p(E_x\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}_{\mathcal O_{X,x}}}M)\) is exact on the category of \(\mathcal O_{X,x}\)-modules (since \(\mathcal O_{X,x}=k(x)\)), hence (5.5 (iii)), there exists an open neighborhood \(U\) of \(x\) such that the functor \(M\longmapsto H^p(E|U\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}}M)\) is exact on \(\operatorname{Mod}(U)\), and, on \(U\), the function

\[ y\longmapsto\operatorname{rg}_{k(x)} H^p(E\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}_X}k(y)) \]

is locally constant by (5.5 (ii)). The constructibility assertion of (i) follows at once. The other assertions follow from (5.5 (ii) and (iii)) and (5.2 (iv)), except the converse of (ii), for which it suffices, after reducing to \(E\) strictly perfect (after localization and truncation), to apply (EGA III 7.6.2 and 7.6.5) to the functor \(T_{-p}=H^p(E\otimes\,\cdot\,)\).

Proposition 5.7. (EGA III 7.7.5 et 7.9.4).

Let \(f:X\longrightarrow Y\) be a proper morphism of quasi-compact schemes, \(E\in\operatorname{ob}D^b(f)_{\mathrm{parf}}\) (4.2), and \(p\in\mathbb Z\). Then, subject to the validity of (2.1), hence for example if \(f\) is projective or if \(Y\) is noetherian:

(i) For \(y\in Y\), the \(H^i(Rf_*(E)\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}_Y}k(y))\) are vector spaces of finite rank over \(k(y)\), zero except for a finite number, and the function

\[ y\longmapsto\sum(-1)^i\operatorname{rg}_{k(y)} H^i(Rf_*(E)\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}_Y}k(y)) \]

is locally constant on \(Y\).

(ii) The function

\[ y\longmapsto\operatorname{rg}_{k(y)} H^p(Rf_*(E)\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}_Y}k(y)) \]

is constructible and upper semi-continuous on \(Y\).

(iii) Suppose \(Y\) quasi-separated. Then, if the functor \(M\longmapsto R^pf_*(E\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}_Y}M)\) is exact on the category of quasi-coherent \(\mathcal O_Y\)-Modules, \(R^pf_*(E)\) is a locally free \(\mathcal O_Y\)-Module of finite type, and the function of (ii) is locally constant. Conversely, if \(Y\) is noetherian and reduced, and if the function of (ii) is locally constant, the functor \(M\longmapsto R^pf_*(E\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}_Y}M)\) is exact on the category of quasi-coherent \(\mathcal O_Y\)-Modules.

Proof. By (4.8), \(Rf_*(E)\) is a perfect complex. Assertion (i) (resp. (ii)) thus follows from (5.5 (i)) (resp. (5.6 (i))). Finally, if \(M\) is a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal O_Y\)-Module, the canonical arrow

\[ Rf_*(E)\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}_Y}M \longrightarrow Rf_*(E\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}_Y}M) \]

is an isomorphism by (3.7), so assertion (iii) follows from (5.6 (ii)).

Remarks 5.8.

a) In the situation of (5.7), let us denote by \(i_y:X_y\longrightarrow X\) the injection of the fiber of \(X\) above a point \(y\) of \(Y\). One can show (cf. (EGA III 6 and IV 3.1.0)) that for \(f\) flat (resp. \(E\) bounded with quasi-coherent and \(Y\)-flat components) we have the Künneth formula:

\[ H^i(Rf_*(E)\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}_Y}k(y)) \mathrel{\widetilde\longrightarrow}H^i(X_y,E_y), \]

where \(E_y=Li_y^*(E)\) (resp. \(i_y^*(E)\)).

b) One can develop for complex analytic spaces an apparatus analogous to that of this section (exchange, semi-continuity and continuity properties)(1). The essential ingredients are: 1) the finiteness theorem for proper morphisms ("Grauert's theorem"); 2) an analogue of the projection formula (3.7), which can be stated as follows: let \(f:X\longrightarrow Y\) be a morphism of analytic spaces, where \(X\) is assumed to be of bounded dimension, and let \(E\in\operatorname{ob}D^-(X)\), \(M\in\operatorname{ob}D^-(Y)_{\mathrm{coh}}\) ("coh" means "with coherent cohomology"). The canonical arrow

\[ Rf_*(E)\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}}M \longrightarrow Rf_*(E\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}_Y}M) \]

is an isomorphism (the hypothesis on \(X\) ensures (T.F II 5.12) that \(f_*\) is of finite cohomological dimension, hence that \(Rf_*\) is defined on \(D(X)\)).

Unlike statement 1), statement 2) is essentially trivial, and follows from a dévissage analogous to that of the proof of (3.7); one has moreover more elbow room since \(Rf_*\) is defined on \(D(X)\), in particular no hypothesis on \(E\) is needed (apart from \(E\in\operatorname{ob}D^-(X)\)).
